okay, so http://www.kvirc.net/doc/doc_dcc_connection.html states:
```
client A sends blocks of data (usually 1-2 KB) and at every block awaits confirmation from the client B, that when receiving a block should reply 4 bytes containing an positive number specifying the total size of the file received up to that moment.
```
and http://www.irchelp.org/protocol/dccspec.html states:
```
The recipient should acknowledge each packet by transmitting the total number of bytes received as an unsigned, 4 byte integer in network byte order.
```
finally, according to https://docs.python.org/2/library/struct.html "!I" is the correct format too, so everything seems to be in order and I'll have to repeat my experiments...

This *patched* it for me on version 5, would not be backwards compatible, but works seemigly for nose:

```python

def _init_pdb(context=3):
    term = import_module(['IPython.terminal.interactiveshell'], "TerminalInteractiveShell")
    debugger_cls = term.TerminalInteractiveShell().debugger_cls
    if 'context' in getargspec(Pdb.__init__)[0]:
        p = debugger_cls(def_colors, context=context)
    else:
        p = debugger_cls(def_colors)
    p.rcLines += def_exec_lines
    return p
```

We're actually talking about two different things.

Using `language: python`, we can test automatically on a number of versions (say, 2.6, 2.7, 3.3 to 3.5 plus an often-updated 3.6 nightly), but only on Linux and in a virtualenv. It should be possible to break out of the virtualenv, but I wouldn't trust that `/usr/bin/python` (or `python3`) be the same version as in the virtualenv. Sure, we can test and find out *where* are those versions, but of course that would be undocumented and subject to change.

On the other hand, using `language: generic` as PGI does, we could test outside a virtualenv (even switching into one if needed), in both Ubuntu and OSX, but we'd be using the versions of Python provided by the OS.

When you add the project, I'll make a PR with a couple tests just to see what version are the system Python installs, where's the original for the versions in the virtualenvs and so on.

Oh. Interesting.  I've actually been bothered a lot by timeout handling in the first version.   Partly it's the fact that every single blocking operation can timeout.  If I put a timeout option on everything, it makes the API unnecessarily complicated.  Also, there's the problem of a cumulative timeout as your describe.

In the current "work in progress" curio, I've taken away the individual timeout features and replaced them with a new call that can work on a single operation like this:

     await timeout_after(5, some_coroutine(...))

Alternatively, you can apply it to a whole block of statements as a context manager

    async with timeout_after(5):
             statements
             ...

This latter form is basically the same as the timeout() manager you have in your code (although implemented as part of the kernel itself).    I've used the name "timeout_after" to maybe make it more clear that the timeout is a cumulative timeout as opposed to a per-operation timeout.

Is it curio a replacement for asyncio that has the features required to do useful things now?  Yes.  Is it stable?  No.  Everything about it is subject to change at any time.  

Looking at it realistically, curio is basically a one-person project and it's using async/await in a completely different way than what was probably originally envisioned.   I think it's still appropriate to call it "experimental" for now.  It gives the project more space to try new ideas.  It also reflects the fact that to be truly usable, curio would need some other libraries for supporting useful protocols like HTTP.

@njsmith AMQP is asynchronous (unlike HTTP). Various events may (or may not) happen at some point in future, when the broker decides to reply back (or not), and there have to be a way to respond to such delayed events.
The use of futures is hidden from the user's point of view - I could provide a callback-based api just fine, but I don't want to.

On 2/1/17, David Beazley <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> Is the purpose of this to somehow integrate Curio into a foreign event loop?

Yes it can be used that way and it can be used to to delegate some
functionality to curio in "existing" applications.

> Can threads not be used instead?

Yes, but virtues of single threaded application is also
great. Isn't ironic that a library targeting to reduce
dependency on threads recommends threading
as the only means of integration/embedding?

Any sane developer would use it at last resort.
Yes, I write this as I'm more comfortable with threads
than most, yet avoid them whenever possible.

The solution wouldn't be rewriting existing apps
in curio, or employing queues all over.. Not to
mention trying to visuliaze and avoid races.

You have above troubles on one hand, and
"Just calling parent app functions
from your coroutines without worrying about thread safety."
on the other. Which one is preferable?

Pushing for curio threads increases barrier of entry for
curio embedding. And enforces unnecessary complexity.

I got your point: For large scale we may not avoid threads.
But for majority of cases single threaded embedding is
the logical solution and will do a prety good job.

> On the whole, I'm not sure I'm so keen on building a whole bunch of
> pause/resume/continue functionality into Curio because before long it's
> likely to turn into a whole menagerie of "run_now", "run_soon", "run_at",
> "run_after", "run_before", "run_whenever",  "run_away", and
> "run_away_faster" functions.

Nobody wants it that fast..

> I suppose one could add some kind of option to the existing `run()` function
> that accepted a number of scheduling cycles to execute. Calling `run()` with
> no arguments currently resumes any tasks that are active.  Would need to
> think about it some more.

To sum up:
- Only pause() is needed. (If run() can cover continuing.)
- This will enable single threaded curio embedding. (Currently it cant be done)

This issue is still valid, as for determining the correct address for sending messages, we need to fetch the module id. This should be done automatically, when starting the communicator. After that, it should be used as the base of every message send. If required, the device could be sent a `Sender ID` derived from the `Base ID`, but `Destination ID` should always be set to the correct device address.
This does require testing on hardware, to make sure that setting the `Destination ID` doesn't mess up anything...

Hello @InunoTaishou as regards sunrise/sunset times PyOWM is defaulting to 0 if it is unable to find the sunrise/sunset information in the JSON returned by the OWM API. If you'could mimic the same API call that PyOWM is doing in your specific case, you would be able to understand if sunrise/sunset times actually *are* in the response JSON - and then we could patch PyOWM to read them.

As regards weather forecasts, the OWM API provides either [3-hours granularity forecasts on a 5-day stripe](https://openweathermap.org/forecast5) or [24-hours granularity forecasts on a 16-day stripe](https://openweathermap.org/forecast16). The first could be retrieved using the `owm.three_hours_forecast` method and the latter using the `owm.daily_forecast` method - both are explained in the [usage-examples](https://github.com/csparpa/pyowm/blob/master/pyowm/docs/usage-examples.md) doc that you linked

It actually "makes sense" because the 70-ish ULP band around the expected `t` actually end up in a 1/2 ULP band around `2.125`:

```python
>>> import numpy as np  # 1.12.0
>>> import sympy  # 1.0
>>> import six  # 1.10.0
>>> 
>>> 
>>> correct_input = float.fromhex('0x1.983e62b67adeep-3')
>>> input_spacing = sympy.Rational(np.spacing(correct_input))
>>> correct_input = sympy.Rational(correct_input)
>>> 
>>> correct_output = 2.125
>>> output_spacing = sympy.Rational(np.spacing(correct_output))
>>> correct_output = sympy.Rational(correct_output)
>>> 
>>> half_left = correct_output - output_spacing / 2
>>> half_right = correct_output + output_spacing / 2
>>> 
>>> 
>>> def poly_eval(val):
...     return ((val + 60) * val + 1076) / 512
... 
>>> 
>>> accepted = []
>>> for delta in six.moves.xrange(-200, 200 + 1):
...     curr_input = correct_input + delta * input_spacing
...     curr_output = poly_eval(curr_input)
...     if half_left <= curr_output <= half_right:
...         accepted.append(delta)
... 
>>> 
>>> accepted == list(six.moves.xrange(-67, 67 + 1))
True
```

I've found a decent workaround for the IDEA console limitations with scripts that need ANSI escape sequences.

I'm using the Python plugin to start a "remote debug" session. It waits for a connection from the script that wants to be debugged.

Then I configured an "External Tool" that uses AppleScript (`osascript`) to start a new iTerm tab and run a simple launcher bash script in that new terminal. The launcher activates the virtualenv of my project and sets some environment variables that my python scripts use. And finally launch the script.

When the script launches if it finds a PYDEVD_EGG environment variable, it will import it and connect to the debugger.

Now my **blessed** based python script has full and complete control over the iTerm tab it was launched in, but it's also connected to the **pydevd** IDE debugger too. I get to have my cake and eat it :)

Oh, sorry for being confusing. I think an example would do best. These all work (as documented):
```
hlist = [{'name':'observer', 'value':'ES', 'comment':'who'},
         {'name':'location','value':'CTIO'},
         {'name':'photometric','value':True}]

fits.write(data, header=hlist)
hdr=FITSHDR(hlist)
fits.write(data, header=hdr)
fits[1].write_keys(hlist)
```
However, counterintuitively (to me), this *does not* work:
```
for hdict in hlist:
    fits[1].write_key(**hdict)
```
The problem is that the first argument in `write_key` is `keyname` instead of `name`:
[`def write_key(self, keyname, value, comment="")`](https://github.com/esheldon/fitsio/blob/89e09660048f2c75918710b90b015f260a139e2d/fitsio/fitslib.py#L1280)

My suggested change would be to change this to:
```def write_key(self, name, value, comment="")```

I seem to have resolved the problem--I seem to have not realized until a few minutes ago that `func_path_generator` and `path_transformer` where somewhat redundant as I was using them; the former being used to extract the function name (to base the cassette file name on) when used as a decorator and the latter being used to modify either the name from the former (when used as a decorator) or the name provided as an argument (when used as a context manager).
If I move my `strip_test` into the compose chain in `path_transformer`, I can do away with `func_path_generator` altogether:

```python
def strip_test(s):
    """Strip a leading 'test_' or trailing '_test' from s."""
    ...

sample_vcr = vcr.VCR(
    path_transformer=compose(vcr.VCR.ensure_suffix('.yaml'), ensure_prefix('sample_'), strip_test),
    cassette_library_dir=str(samples_path() / 'vcr')
)

@pytest.fixture
def vcr_cassette(request):
    with sample_vcr.use_cassette(request.function.__name__) as cassette:
        yield cassette
```

Now I can use `@sample_vcr.use_cassette` as a decorator or `vcr_cassette` as a pytest fixture!

The compile arguments on Linux look like this:

`gcc` parameters (outputs object file):

```
-I/usr/local/include -I/usr/include/python2.7 -c tesserocr.cpp -o build/temp.linux-x86_64-2.7/tesserocr.o
```
`/usr/local/include` contains the `tesseract` and `leptonica` folders which contain their respective header files, and `/usr/include/python2.7` contains the Python header files (either Python 2 or 3).

`c++ ` parameters (outputs binary file):

```
-L/usr/local/lib -llept -ltesseract -o tesserocr.so
```
`/usr/local/lib` contains `liblept.so` and `libtesseract.so` (DLLs in your case) while `lept` and `tesseract` are the names of the libraries.

Perhaps you can try translate these parameters in your command (or the VS solution) to try to achieve a successful build. Hope this helps.

downspeed = a2-a1

@edevil Can I just confirm: is this only a problem if you have other middleware that runs before WhiteNoise and opens a db connection? So if WhiteNoise is always the first bit of middleware this problem won't occur, is that right?

Even if support was added to WhiteNoise for listening to these signals, writing files after the application starts seems suboptimal for several reasons:
1) In the case of eg Heroku, the slug has already been built - so any computation has to be repeated for every web dyno & won't be persisted after dyno restart (which happens every 24 hours) or after deploy.
2) For the first time each file is requested on each dyno (and taking into account [1]), the request will take much longer.
3) Presuming the WhiteNoise support was roughly: "if the file doesn't exist, fallback to the Django wsgi app, then catch the signal and add to the WhiteNoise files list for the next request", this still means Django would have to serve the file for that first request, which doesn't work when not using runserver. This would also likely mean inconsistent behaviour between first and second requests for a file (eg headers; hence why we have [the overriding runserver feature](http://whitenoise.evans.io/en/stable/django.html#using-whitenoise-in-development) as-is).

Is there a use-case I'm missing as to why django-compressor's offline mode can't just be used? (Seeing as for development, whitenoise already supports auto-refresh)

Hi @camohub!

Yes, the actual problem is that the `cssIcon` is only applied on initialization. To add an unsorted icon, use the [`cssIconNone` option](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#cssiconnone) instead.

```js
$("#sortTable").tablesorter({
    debug: true,
    dateFormat: 'ddmmyyy',
    sortLocaleCompare: true,
    theme: 'blue',
    cssIcon: 'fa',
    cssIconAsc: 'fa-sort-asc',
    cssIconDesc: 'fa-sort-desc',
    cssIconNone: 'fa-sort',
    headerTemplate: '{content} {icon}'
});
```

Also, note that the [`dateFormat` option](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#dateformat) for this fork does not support `"pt"` as an option. Only the [original tablesorter](https://github.com/christianbach/tablesorter/blob/master/jquery.tablesorter.js#L992) hosted here on GitHub supports that; not even the version you get from [`tablesorter.com`](http://tablesorter.com/) supports this option.

Hi @ivanny!

When the [`refreshWidgets` method](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#refreshwidgets) is used, it removes the widget then re-initializes it. When this method is triggered using "filterEnd", the "filterEnd" event is fired immediately after "filterInit", so it's creating an endless loop.

It would be better to trigger `$(window).resize()` on "filterInit".

Hi @johnnymex!

Within the theme file, every definition starts out with `.tablesorter-default` so as to target only the chosen class name. Make sure to rename all of these css selectors as well.

Might I also suggest an alternative. Although it may seem daunting, test out one of the LESS themes. 

* [Basic Tablesorter theme](http://codepen.io/Mottie/pen/eqBbn)
* [Bootstrap Tablesorter theme](http://codepen.io/Mottie/pen/Ltzpi/)
* [Metro Tablesorter theme](http://codepen.io/Mottie/details/gCslk/)

Rename the theme in two places:

* LESS: `@theme : tablesorter-custom;`
* HTML: `<table class="tablesorter-custom">`

Then tweak the colors until you get a design you like then click on the little eye in the upper right corner to show the compiled css.

Once you copy that css to your editor, you'll need to strip out a few css definitions that don't apply - the first 60 or so lines of the css; the comments and the selectors that don't start with `.tablesoter-{theme}`.

Anyway, [this is the input-select parser](https://github.com/Mottie/tablesorter/blob/master/js/parsers/parser-input-select.js). It is a separate file from the main plugin and needs to be loaded, and the parser for that column needs to be set. The easiest ways to set the parser are by class name:

```html
<th class="sorter-inputs">Foo</th>
```

or in the [`headers` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#headers):

```js
headers : {
    0 : { sorter: 'inputs' }
}
```

Yes. `url` module in `NodeJS`: https://www.npmjs.com/package/url `resolve()` is more than just `urllib.parse.urljoin()`. It resolves a target URL relative to a base URL in a manner similar to that of a Web browser resolving an anchor tag HREF. Examples: 1. `Go home` => change `path` + clear `query` + clear `fragment`. 2. `Page 1` => clear `query` + clear `fragment`. Very useful for HTML parsing.

Running `subprocess.call(shlex.split("python -c 'print \"quoted args in here\"'"))` does work, but envoy fails. I've dug into it, and I've found that `shlex.shlex` in the expand_args function is dropping the `'` quotes. This is due to the whitespace change. 

As I noted earlier, the quotes are removed because each item is supposed to go into it's own spot in the list. But that is based upon whitespace, which is only considered to be `|` characters. So we get:
```
>>> splitter = shlex.shlex("python -c 'print \"quoted args here\"'", posix=True)
>>> splitter.whitespace = '|'
>>> splitter.whitespace_split = True
>>> command = []
>>> token = splitter.get_token()
>>> token
'python -c print "quoted args here"'
```

One token, because there is no "whitespace". Then, when shlex.split gets mapped onto this item, it does not have posix mode set, but that does not matter because it sees `'python -c print "quoted args here"'` as it's input string. 

What this is supposed to do is split on valid `|` characters, but it is doing more than that as well it seems. Just using string split won't work, because the `|` could be inside of quotes, or comments.

I've created a *very* dirty fix for this where it iterates over the input string and accumulates the cmds between pipes. It needs to be tested for weird quoting like `cmd " args \" other args \" " | do things` which would probably break it.

I've added it to my repo, and opened a new pull request.

the problem with this particular test is the number of significant digits reported by stable32. It rounds the "n" number to 5 significant digits, so there is usually an error if n>1e5.
checking for the exact match n_1 == n_2 is very useful when searching for bugs in new algorithms or optimizations - I think the test should be left mostly as it is now.
If possible more significant digits from Stable32 should be obtained - or the test could be relaxed for n>1e5 but this is not so elegant.

> This sounds kinda sensible, but do you also have a concrete use case in mind?

An example in my case is I want to check an input to a function - let's say it's called `inpf` - to see if a SoundFile was passed in or if `inpf` is just the default `None` value. It's an easy fix, I just have to check `if inpf is None` instead of `if inpf`. So I would consider this is a very minor issue, but I still think most users would see this as surprising behavior.

> But there might still be some edge cases where the reported length is garbage.

I don't know about the write case, but in the read case I sometimes need to pipe input from different audio applications. (Think reading from an audio stream of unspecified origin). If I try opening STDIN as input to a SoundFile without a specified length (the format being RAW or AU) I get a garbage `__len__` of `2**60 - 1` on my particular 64bit linux box. (Just FYI, to open stdin I pass in sys.stdin.fileno() or just the string '-'). 

I tend to agree that the "right" solution would be to drop __len__ altogether. But at this point it's a matter of 'is it worth changing it?'. On one side, SoundFiles more often than not have a sensible __len__ which has a consistent meaning (number of frames). On the other, bool() of an empty file evaluates to False (odd), and in a small minority of cases `len()` returns a garbage value.

```Python
>>> i = pretty_midi.Instrument(0, 0)
>>> i.notes.append(pretty_midi.Note(100, 0, 1., 3.))
>>> i.notes.append(pretty_midi.Note(100, 5, 3.01, 4.))
>>> i.get_pitch_class_transition_matrix(True)[5, 0]
1.0
```
Is this the standard way of doing it?  It would be a little more intuitive to me if it was transposed, so that `pitch_class_transition_matrix[i, j]` is the number or proportion of transitions from pitch class `i` to pitch class `j`. My main goal was is to store migrations within the application. This is most easily done by providing them in as proper python modules. It simplifies the setup.py and makes it easy to access the migrations after the module has been installed from any python module.

Also because they are modules they do not need to be parsed and run... This makes debugging a migration just like any other python module as well as quickly highlights import errors and other runtime things.

Also not that it matters too much but you can import logging or other modules and they should behave as expected since you are operating within the standard python execution flow.

### Simplified example:
**Assume**:
myapp/migrations/__init__.py
myapp/migrations/0_1_0.py
myapp/migrations/0_2_0.py
myapp/migrations/0_6_1.py

myapp/migrate.py
```python
from peewee import *
from peewee_migrate.router import ModuleRouter
DB = Proxy()

DB.initialize(connect('sqlite:///mydb.db'))
router = Router(DB, migrate_module='myapp.migrations')

for migration in ('0_1_0', '0_2_0', '0_6_1'):
    router.run(migration)
```
### More verbose example:

You are trying to put too much intelligence where it simply doesn't belong.

The broker sees 2 data streams and doesn't make any assumption about them being related. Your problem is that you know they are related and you expect the world to also know about and act accordingly. But for the broker the streams are unrelated.

If you tell the broker to buy from `self.data0` (which is the default) it will buy from it at the available price. The broker is doing what it is meant to do.

The instruction to buy from `self.data0` has been given by the strategy and it is the responsible to issue orders: for which data. The strategy is the only relevant part in this behavior.

I have had this exception before, and it warranted immediate attention and a fix to the server setup. Without this exception, the resulting 500 error, the resulting email notification, this error would have gone on unnoticed until a customer / client / user notified us, which is unacceptable. If you are interacting with another library that is messing with files you should wrap those interactions with your own try catch, as you would be able to best handle the exception there instead of declaring that this exception is no longer *exceptional* because of an edge case. I am a strong NO on an PR for ignoring this exception.

Perhaps instead we can read the return value from `cleanup_pre_delete` and if we receive a `False` value we do not proceed with the delete and the `cleanup_post_delete` would not be called. But if the return value is 'None' or 'True' we continue as usual. Then in `cleanup_pre_delete` someone can determine if the file can be deleted or not. We may need to send additional information in the signal, such as the model and field being operated on.

I think there are multiple options:

* Use doxygendiagram pending feature to display inheritance graph
* An indented item list with the name of each class including an internal link if the class is "defined" somewhere else.

Maybe it would be necessary to have an option to limit inheritance level. I mean, if A inherit of B which inherit of C maybe we only want to show that A inherit of B.

In parallel to this feature, sometimes it's useful to comment members of a class A inherited from the class B and not the class B. However, to document inherited members from class B, it's necessary to use class B syntax (B::member) which is not easily comprehensible if we don't specify that A inherit from B.
For example, it would be nice to have something like

.. doxygenfunction:: A::memberFromClassA
.. doxygenfunction:: A::memberInheritedFromClassB

Thank you,

Guillaume

Okay so then my opinion about namespaced packages... although I mostly just want a bug fixed :)

Firstly, thanks for the thoughtful response and elaboration. I understand now how namespaced packages have evolved into a strategy and method for distributing smaller chunks of code, with the potential to better maintain and optimize popularly shared code fragments. I'm very much against these huge `utils` or `shared` monolith libraries, so I get why this can be nicer.

On the more optimistic side, I'd say that these popular code fragments rarely cause problems. Don't remember encountering any myself. For instance `lru_cache`, I always just copy-pasted from Django. Never had issues. This function was then finally added to `functools` in Python 3, moreover it never had any real maintenance for the 4 years it sat in the Django repo. This could also be the path for `@classproperty`.

Would you have wanted Django to add a dependency for `lru_cache` in 2013-2017? I would think they were out of their minds if they added a dependency for this small function, giving all the test responsibility and security risks implied to 1 person. See its short history here: https://github.com/django/django/commits/master/django/utils/lru_cache.py

For this distribution mechanism to work in principle, there would have to be more active maintainers. Otherwise it's a gaping security hole with lots of bus factor. You can't have lots of small namespaced packages in large, popular projects if they aren't nursed. Consider maybe talking to jazzband.co about some community-driven utility namespace.

Lemme know if the docs could be more clear (currrently `wormhole rx --help` is the only place that reveals this option, but when we get some longer-form prose docs written up, I think "How To Use Magic-Wormhole In Automation Scripts" might be a good section, and `--output-file=` would make a prominent appearance in there).

The upstream bug was filed on behalf of git-annex, which sets `LANG=C` to prevent any potential I18N translations of the output messages from changing, so `wormhole` can be run as a child script (with `git-annex` reading its output). I think `LANG=C.UTF-8` is morally equivalent, but @joeyh 's report suggests that `LANG=C` has been tradition for a long time, and `C.UTF-8` isn't quite as likely to be available as `C`.

I suppose the assumption is that `LANG=C` will get you pure-ASCII output messages, so there's no need for a UTF-8 capable terminal. Click doesn't know that we're going to avoid this, and in fact (as y'all pointed out) non-ASCII filenames will get printed regardless of which language we use for progress messages, so I guess we really *do* require a UTF-8 capable terminal.

    except ProgrammingError:
        print 'Database already exists'
    engine.execute('USE testdb')
    Base.metadata.create_all(bind=engine)
    db_session.configure(bind=engine)
    request.addfinalizer(lambda: database_teardown(url))

    order = Order(device=Device())
    db_session.add(order)
    db_session.flush()

This may be a bit late, but I'm adding this here to help anyone who is still having trouble filtering by groups. To retrieve the group in Active Directory you need to use the following format:
`(memberOf=CN=GroupName,CN=Users,DC=example,DC=com)`

In python 2.7x, the format_search_filters converts the ldap_fields string to Unicode, replacing the `= `with its Unicode value `\\3D` This causes the lookup to fail. You can work around it by appending the AD string to the search_filter after the format_search_filter function is called.
e.g. `search_filters.append('(memberOf=CN=GroupName,CN=Users,DC=example,DC=com)')`

This will return all users that are members of GroupName in the domain OU you specified.

I think that your solution is to specify a common ancestor of the search
tree, then add some search filters, like this:

https://github.com/etianen/django-python3-ldap#custom-user-filters

The username and password are set by the details the user provides when they log in. That is, you don’t log into the LDAP server using a fixed user and password, but rather you log in with the user’s own user account.

The ldap_sync_users command currently assumes anonymous access to the user listing is allowed. Allowing this to take a username and password would be a useful feature.

> On ext4, when `auto_da_alloc` is enabled(default on) it looks like removing the fsync for file is safe too:
> 
> ```
> auto_da_alloc(*)	Many broken applications don't use fsync() when 
> noauto_da_alloc		replacing existing files via patterns such as
> 			fd = open("foo.new")/write(fd,..)/close(fd)/
> 			rename("foo.new", "foo"), or worse yet,
> 			fd = open("foo", O_TRUNC)/write(fd,..)/close(fd).
> 			If auto_da_alloc is enabled, ext4 will detect
> 			the replace-via-rename and replace-via-truncate
> 			patterns and force that any delayed allocation
> 			blocks are allocated such that at the next
> 			journal commit, in the default data=ordered
> 			mode, the data blocks of the new file are forced
> 			to disk before the rename() operation is
> 			committed.  This provides roughly the same level
> 			of guarantees as ext3, and avoids the
> 			"zero-length" problem that can happen when a
> 			system crashes before the delayed allocation
> 			blocks are forced to disk.
> ```
>

I'm not 100% sure, but it is quite possible that the problem is that you are mixing eventlet with regular Python threads. This isn't a good idea, the green threads that eventlet uses do not work well with regular thread. Instead, you should use green threads for all your threading needs.

One option is to monkey patch the Python standard library, so that threading, sockets, etc. are replaced with eventlet friendly versions. You can do this at the very top of your script:

    import eventlet
    eventlet.monkey_patch()

After looking at it closer, it seems that by default, the recommonmark parser also treat <...> as html tags. Docs I've found on Markdown also seem to go this way. The only way to make it be parsed as < is to put a space after it, or to escape it with <. So this pull request does not seem to be justified. Thanks and sorry !

Note that the `ignore` option doesn't support end-of-line comments either. However, the way its value is split up, `W293, # blank line contains whitespace` for example tells flake8 to ignore any error code that starts with `W239`, `#`, `blank`, `line`, `contains` or `whitespace`. While the additional tokens don't have any effect in this example (since no valid error code can start with any of them), it can cause unintended effects in other cases, e.g. `Z100 # is redundant with Y200` will cause both `Z100` and `Y200` to be ignored.

The approach is very common. It's the only way of doing custom checkboxes (or radio buttons). Googling for "custom checkbox" gives the following results:

https://codepen.io/CreativeJuiz/pen/BiHzp
https://css-tricks.com/snippets/css/custom-checkboxes-and-radio-buttons/
http://webdesign.tutsplus.com/articles/quick-tip-easy-css3-checkboxes-and-radio-buttons--webdesign-8953

... all of which use this technique. I do a lot of frontend work and have seen those quite a lot. Why a `label` element and not a let's say `span`? Because normally clicking the label will also toggle the checkbox. Nevertheless: in order to make the checkboxes togglable, each checkbox would need an `id="xyz"` and the label would need a`for= "xyz"`. However, the checkboxes are all disabled, so they aren't clickable anyway, so it's just for styling purposes. If those task list elements SHOULD be clickable in the future (with some background action triggering, regardless of the sense in that), oe would only need to generate ids for the checkboxes and adjust the labels, so it's even future proof.

And yes, browsers suck. It works in Chrome by just defining a pseudo element on the checkbox, but it's not specified so technically it's undefined behaviour.

Regarding setattr for parameterdict, it should work if we always want to change the attributes of all parameters. I am wondering if there is a case in which the user may want to create a Block, that internally uses (lets say) 2 Blocks but each of them has a different grad_req.

In that case, with the API introduced by this PR, the user may add 2 parameters to the init function of his block, and pass the respective one to the Blocks that he uses to compose his block.

On the other hand for setattr one could introduce a prefix filter, that sets only the attributes of parameters whose name starts with the given prefix, which would result in the same behaviour.

below is the benchmark script,

```
import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np
import time

mx.random.seed(123)
np.random.seed(123)

out_dims = 1024
batch_size = 1000

for i in range(1, 3):
    fea_dims = 1000000 * i
    density = 0.001 / i
    rshape = (fea_dims, out_dims)
    lshape = (batch_size, fea_dims)
    csr = mx.test_utils.rand_ndarray(lshape, 'csr', density=density)
    dns = mx.test_utils.rand_ndarray(rshape, 'default')
    inds = np.unique(csr.indices.asnumpy())
    print len(inds)
    rsp = mx.nd.sparse.retain(dns.tostype('row_sparse'), mx.nd.array(inds))
    print len(rsp.indices)
    # warm up
    for i in range(5):
        mx.nd.dot(csr, rsp)
    mx.nd.waitall()

    tic = time.time()
    for i in range(30):
        mx.nd.dot(csr, rsp)
    mx.nd.waitall()
    print time.time() - tic
```

Following @minrk  and @D3f0's comments, I got this to work for `Django==1.6, ipython==3.0.0, django-extensions==1.5.2` as follows:

```
# add this to your ipython_config
# (typically ~/.ipython/profile_default/ipython_config.py)
import os
if os.environ.get('DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE'):
  c.InteractiveShellApp.extensions = ['django_extensions.management.notebook_extension']
```

This way `manage.py shell_plus [--notebook]`  works as before with IPython 2.4, and IPython also works without Django. It's a hack, but it works, follows DRY & is less verbose than running %load_ext in every notebook.

Sorry about that. I should have mentioned that it is a change in Python as well. Python has adopted this convention, and I applied Python's own behavior to pyzmq in 14.7.

```python
import time
import signal
import sys

print("Current Python version is %s" % sys.version.split()[0])

def signal_handler(signum, frame):
    sys.stdout.write(".")
    sys.stdout.flush()

signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal_handler)
tic = time.time()
time.sleep(5)
print('%.1f' % (time.time()-tic))
```

@SylvainCorlay If you did that you'd need to make sure the method was popped off the state in `__getstate__` to maintain pickleability. That harkens back to this notion of `getstate` and `setstate` methods on descriptors proposed by @NeilGirdhar in #94.

And making the default handler dict a class attribute won't work without creating it in the metaclass because it isn't something that should be shared with subclasses. That would then cause problems in the cases where `HasDescriptors` was not the base class.

I don't think provisional warnings / context managers solve any real problems. Either people will use provisional features, in which case they will be just as frustrated if things break (being declared 'provisional' doesn't really change that), or (probably more likely) they won't be used until they make it into a stable release, in which case the provisional stuff may as well not exist until the provisional state is removed.

The ``cli4`` command uses : to introduce elements that need processing. In the [README](https://github.com/cloudflare/python-cloudflare/blob/master/README.md) file, this is described within some of the examples.

You were doing this correctly; however, the ``/zones/:zone_id/logs/received`` & ``/zones/:zone_id/logs/received/fields`` API calls don't return data in the same format as the existing v4 calls. This causes ``cli4`` (actually the underlying libraries) to fail when parsing the returned JSON. This is not good. Code needs to be written in order to address this. I will do that soon.

As a side note, your effort to add these API calls via ``$HOME/.cloudflare/cloudflare.cfg`` would have the same issue. The return values would cause the underlying libraries to raise an error.

The [document for glacier client's complete_multipart_upload()](http://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/glacier.html#Glacier.Client.complete_multipart_upload) is incorrect in this case. You will have to provide the checksum explicitly. The good news is there is already a (undocumented?) helper for you.

    from botocore.utils import calculate_tree_hash

    client.complete_multipart_upload(
        checksum=calculate_tree_hash(open('your_file.txt')),
        vaultName=vault_name, uploadId=upload_id, archiveSize=str(fsize))

By the way, same error also applies to the [documentation for the MultipartUpload resource's complete() method](http://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/glacier.html#Glacier.MultipartUpload.complete).

    multipart_upload = glacier.MultipartUpload(account_id, VAULT_NAME, upload_id)

    multipart_upload.complete(
        checksum=calculate_tree_hash(open('file.txt')),

I added some line notes but I also have one bigger question about the usage. What is the point to allow returning either an object or a dictionary? An object with all information in easily accessible attributes is much more useful when just querying info. What use cases are there for returning a dict?

If returning a dict is a useful feature, then it would probably be a good idea to always return a dictionary but use custom dict that allows accessing keys as attributes like `${dict.key}`. RF 2.9 added [DotDict](https://github.com/robotframework/robotframework/blob/master/src/robot/utils/dotdict.py) that behaves that way. S2L could use it directly, but it would make it dependent on RF 2.9. I don't think that is generally acceptable, but perhaps only this kw could require it. Alternatively S2L could include its own version. It probably wouldn't need to be ordered and thus wouldn't need Py 2.6 compatible `OrderedDict`.

@gerhc ,

No problem!

In the new `rio-color@colorspaces` branch, I added two functions to do direct conversions to and from LCH and RGB color space. 

* [`rgb_to_lch`](https://github.com/mapbox/rio-color/blob/colorspaces/rio_color/colorspace.py#L5)
* [`lch_to_rgb`](https://github.com/mapbox/rio-color/blob/colorspaces/rio_color/colorspace.py#L90)

The eventual plan is to port them to Cython or C, and vectorize them for use with numpy arrays.  But for now, let's work with pure functions in Python to make sure we get the math right.

@virginiayung, I could use your 👀  and 💭  on the above functions. I mainly followed the wikipedia pages for [sRGB](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SRGB) and [LAB](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lab_color_space) in order to translate the formulas to Python. 

It works reasonably well but when you round trip from rgb -> lch -> rgb repeatedly, you get some minor floating-point drift and I'm not sure if it's due to an error in my formulas or just normal float imprecision.

/cc @ericfischer @celoyd

@celoyd approaching this from another angle - the performance penalty of using skimage to convert to LCH colorspace is significant. Using a naive numpy implementation to convert RGB to HSL color space is roughly 75x faster and allows us to drop skimage as a dependency. 

How much better is the saturation adjustment in the LCH space? Significant enough from HSL to justify backwards incompatibility with our existing formulas, a performance penalty and a large dependency? I'm leaning *no* and would like to explore using HSL/HSV...

A few options:

1. use LCH, deal with slight change vs imagemagick and keep skimage dependency
2. use LCH, deal with slight change vs imagemagick and experiment with implementing our own LCH conversion
3. use HSL, compatible with imagemagick, fast numpy implementation is easy to implement

Still experiencing this issue, fresh virtualenv and setuptools reinstallation did not help.

I did some digging into this issue; it's either a setuptools or distutils problem. I'll stick to cpplint, but any package using setuptools and entry points should show this behaviour. Things I have observed:

1) `pip install cpplint` installs the wheel and works fine.
2) `pip install --no-binary cpplint cpplint` **doesn't** quote the shebang properly. It installs from sdist by running `setup.py install --single-version-externally-managed` locally.
3) `setup.py install` from the source installs fine.
4) `setup.py install --single-version-externally-managed` from the source **doesn't** quote the shebang line properly.

I don't know what the egg installation code does different, but in [setuptools/command/install_scripts.py line 33](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/install_scripts.py#L33) `exec_param` is retrieved from the build_scripts command which in turn gets it from the build command (`distutils/command/build_scripts.py` line 38) which, if unspecified, sets it to this (`distutils/command/build.py line 120):

    self.executable = os.path.normpath(sys.executable)

Then, `exec_param` -- which is just an unquoted path, possibly containing spaces -- gets passed to `CommandSpec.from_param` ([setuptools/command/install_scripts.py line 42](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/install_scripts.py#L42)) which parses it as a command line by splitting it along spaces ([setuptools/command/easy_install.py line 1954](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L1954)). As a consequence, the special quoting code gets bypassed.

Wrapping `exec_param` in a one-element list solved the problem (not quite a one-line fix because it shouldn't be wrapped if it's `None`). I can roll a PR, but I don't know the setuptools codebase well enough to determine if this is the best place to fix this.

Changing the method to this seems to fix the issue by sidestepping `inspect.getmro`:

```python
def get_unpatched_class(cls):
    """Protect against re-patching the distutils if reloaded
    Also ensures that no other distutils extension monkeypatched the distutils
    first.
    """
    external_bases = (
        cls
        for cls in (cls,) + cls.__bases__
        if not cls.__module__.startswith('setuptools')
    )
    base = next(external_bases)
    if not base.__module__.startswith('distutils'):
        msg = "distutils has already been patched by %r" % cls
        raise AssertionError(msg)
    return base
```

I'm not sure if this introduces any other issues. `inspect.getmro` seems to just return a list of all base classes (including the class itself), sorted by the MRO. `cls.__bases__` also seems to do this, except it doesn't include the class itself.

This looks great! Thanks @dstufft and @ionelmc.

I am pretty sure there will be regressions here - virtualenv is chock-full of ugly special-case fixes for ugly special cases (OS, distribution, etc). A parallel release strategy should help uncover those. I noted one related to globally-installed eggs. Hopefully the new simplified `site.py` and introspection strategy will reduce the number of those, and the modularity will allow pushing off more of the fixes onto the OS/distribution/Python that needs special-casing.

A few years ago, I'd have been sad to see virtualenv lose its single-file-ness (usable without installing), but today I think that's less important, since virtualenv is rapidly becoming just a convenience/legacy shim around the built-in `venv` module.

Same goes for having dependencies, though I'd still be in the camp of preferring to see the `click` dependency removed. It just doesn't bring enough value to justify the shift from "no dependencies" to "has dependencies", IMO.

Yes, I'd like to restrict the version of the API calls that pykafka uses. It's less about exactly 0.9 protocol vs 0.10, but instead a few specific API calls that have multiple versions. 

The issue that I'm trying to avoid is this: https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#upgrade_10_performance_impact

We upgraded our Kafka clusters from 0.8.2.1 to 0.10.0.1, but most of our consumers use older libraries that do not support the new message format. So we set the brokers' `log.message.format.version` to 0.8.2 while we work on upgrading our consumers. 

In the meantime, I'm worried about a performance problem with consumers that have upgraded and are requesting messages using the new Kafka 10 versions of those API calls. I may be misunderstanding, but it seems logical that the performance problem isn't just about old consumers with a new broker. I would think the performance problem would also exist if we take new brokers, set the log format to an old version, then have our consumers make API calls using the Kafka 10 API calls. The broker would need to do on-the-fly conversion from the 0.8.2 log format up to the 0.10.0 format to send to the new consumers. So as we upgraded consumers, we were planning to pin them to old API versions until all our code was upgraded, then we could just flip the broker log format version and the consumer's broker API version at the same time.

After further investigation, I found that the culprit seems to be that the Control Sequence Introducer sent back is `'\x9b'` instead of `'\x1b['` (ESC + [). [This Wikipedia paragraph](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C0_and_C1_control_codes#Encoding_interoperability) suggests that for interoperability with Unicode, using the 7-bit compatible C0 sequence `'\x1b['` will be more robust than the single character C1 code.

If I change CSI in pyte.control to `'\x1b['`, my problem above goes away - both the device attributes and device status requests are sent back into the terminal successfully.

> Recently, @lagru committed to scipy scipy/scipy#8264 a new function to detect peaks based on their prominence. This function is particularly easy to use and efficient for 1D data. Then, it would be worth to consider whether it can be generalized to 2D data. @lagru, would you like to share your expertise on this and eventually contribute to scikit-image if you wish and if it makes sense to you? -- @sciunto 

First of all, I'm always happy to help and learn something new in the process. I already thought about whether the function in question [_argmaxima1d](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/master/scipy/signal/_peak_finding_utils.pyx#L16-L79) could be generalized to the 2D case. I haven't tried to implement it yet but I think it might be possible by using it in a two step algorithm:

1. Consider only the 1D case use the existing algorithm to find all local maxima in each row.
2. Evaluate each maxima in the other direction. How this evaluation (diagonal / direct neighbors) might look would be something to figure out. 

This should't be too hard (I think?) because it's at actually a very straightforward approach. This could work for flat maxima as well and return all positions in the plateau. 
I'll think on it and play around with it. However I haven't looked at the solutions already present in skimage and whether this would have any advantages over the existing approaches. I'll report back if I find anything useful to add.

Evaluating the prominence of maxima is an entirely different beast in this context and unfortunately I don't think my approach can be generalized to the 2D case in an easy way. 

Furthermore it would be useful to agree on a definition and terminology on how a peak prominence is measured before moving on. What I used for the 1D case is actually derived from the 2D case: [topographic prominence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topographic_prominence). That basically requires an algorithm that calculates the prominence by finding the lowest contour line for each peak in question. The [1D algorithm](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/master/scipy/signal/_peak_finding_utils.pyx#L154-L246) might be useful as a step in that but I currently have no idea how to build on that for the 2D case.

---

> Flat peaks? Oh, ok, it highlights all pixels. Or: Flat peaks? Oh, ok, it highlights the center of the peak. -- @stefanv 

> Both of those are valid options, and the documentation should specify what each algorithm delivers. No included algorithm should ever return a value that is not in a maximum or maximum plateau though, imho. -- @jni 

For the 1D case we decided to approximate the center of the flat peak and return that. But in the future one could provide a keyword to control that behavior.
However I don't think this maps well to the 2D space. A solution might be to return all samples in a flat peak as the default case and extend on that by providing several ways / functions that can approximate a center.

---

That's an interesting read @ThomasWalter. I'll try to chime in with my perspective coming from the simpler 1D space.

> The only uncertainty in this definition is thus what happens with an entirely constant image (I would just say, that in this case, there is no maximum).

This can be solved if we use read the definition for peaks like this: A peak **must** be surrounded on all sides by smaller values. For a flat image this is obviously never the case. That's how `find_peaks` handles the 1D case.

> Some people also want to use distance to other local maxima. This is of course also an ambiguous criterion, as if two or more local maxima are too close to each other, it is unclear which to remove.

I solved that ambiguity partly by assigning a priority to each peak. Currently this priority is based on the height of each peak meaning smaller peaks are removed until all remaining peaks fulfill the distance criterion. However if the priority value is the same for two peaks the ambiguity resurfaces. 
[The way I implemented it](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/master/scipy/signal/_peak_finding_utils.pyx#L82-L151) it's possible to use other peak properties to define the priority which I plan to add in the future.

---

On a final note, I think a simple peak finding function for the 2D case might actually be more useful as an addition to SciPy instead of scikit-image. But that's not really an important decision.

This is a followup to this pull request: https://github.com/pydata/patsy/pull/67 

I couldn't figure out how to submit a pull request from my fork of @chrish42 's fork -- seems like I didn't have permission. Anyway, this outlines an approach to pickling, unpickling, and testing.


A few questions for @njsmith and @chrish42 :

1. Location of pickling tests -- do you prefer to have them all in one place, or spread them around? 
   I see both conventions elsewhere in this library. It looks like simple tests are inline with class definitions, and high level tests are all in their own module. I think it makes sense to keep these by themselves, but just wanted to check.
2. I switched away from using tuples to represent state and am using a dict instead.  IMO this will allow better inter-version compatability and is overall safer.
3. I thought about versioning a bit. As I understand it, there are two pieces to think about:
   * At runtime: object gets pickled in one version and unpickled in a different version: 
        * As long as all the expected data is available, this should work fine. 
        * Otherwise, this can be handled by the unpickler on a case-by-case basis.
        * For example, in my implementation this will be extremely verbose, and give either an error or a warning depending on what's missing.
        * And each version gets to decide for itself what data is required vs optional in order to unpickle.
        * I don't think it actually needs to be this verbose for EvalFactor, just wanted to show the way I was thinking about it.
   * At test time: how will we know what whether code changes make it impossible to unpickle older versions, or whether an old version unpickles to something different than we expect?
        * I solved this by just storing a version-by-version history of what each object looks like pickled.
        * This ensures that we'll catch when new versions stop being able to unpickle something that was pickled by an older version.
        * And if we decide to stop supporting an older version, we can just delete it from the history (and add a descriptive message to the unpickling function).
        * There may be a better place to store this data than just inline in the file, but I couldn't think of a good way to do it. Suggestions welcome.
        * Similarly, I'm just generating it now by running it locally and copying it into the file. Again, there may be a better way to do it.
        * This may get cumbersome to maintain, so I'm open to talking about it more.
4. Git history -- I want to acknowledge the work that chrish put in and I did end up using some of his code. So are you okay merging those partial, work in progress commits? Or would you prefer I clean up that history?

I think that covers most of the concerns. Does this approach work for you? Any other ideas for how to do this?

On Sun, Aug 16, 2015 at 2:35 AM, Nathaniel J. Smith <
notifications@github.com> wrote:

> You can use incr_dbuilder
> <https://patsy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/API-reference.html#patsy.incr_dbuilder>
> or incr_dbuilders
> <https://patsy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/API-reference.html#patsy.incr_dbuilders>
> (depending on whether you want to give it y values as well -- it's the
> same distinction as dmatrix versus dmatrices). They're design to work
> with data that's too large to fit into memory, so instead of giving them
> your data directly, you give them a function that returns an iterable over
> "chunks" of your data. If your data fits into memory (just the design
> matrix doesn't), then you could do something simple like:
>
> def iter_maker():
>     return [my_data_frame]
> design_info = incr_dbuilder("a + b + log(x)", iter_maker)
>
> and now design_info is a DesignInfo
> <https://patsy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/API-reference.html#patsy.DesignInfo>
> object with a column_names attribute.
>
> If your raw data is too large to fit into memory, you can change
> iter_maker so that it returns an iterable over chunks of your data where
> each chunk is small enough to fit into memory. Writing a generator is often
> the easiest way to do this -- something like:
>
> def iter_maker():
>     CHUNK_SIZE = 1000
>     offset = 0
>     while offset < total_rows:
>         yield fetch_rows_from_database(offset=offset, size=CHUNK_SIZE)
>
> I'm not sure what use the column names are without the design matrix... I
> would be curious to hear what you're going to do with them :-)
>
> Notice that once you have a DesignInfo, you can use this to generate
> individual "slices" of your design matrix, so you can pass it to algorithms
> that can process design matrix incrementally.
>
> chunk_of_design_matrix = dmatrix(design_info, chunk_of_data)
>
> (NB: this answer assumes you're using patsy 0.4 or greater; everything is
> still possible with earlier versions but 0.4.0 simplified things by
> combining what used to be two different classes into a single DesignInfo
> class, so some details may be different.)
>

Decorator does not work well for this. People may need to write some code to create the arg_sets, e.g. a list comprehension with string formatting. This code needs to be executed somewhere, before setup kicks off, to generate new tests. Decorator does not provide such a venue. The order of setup_class affects how the record is tallied, changing its order requires a lot of changes. setup_class is also the place users register controllers today, whereas all we want is to have a pre-process that decides what tests there are. This is actually a precursor to be able to list all the tests in a test class, which is required for CTS/GTS integration. If generate_tests is mixed into setup_class, it would be impossible to do list tests without also initializing all the controllers. My interpretation of what should happen:

There is a repeated sequence of commands of the form (length:5, dist: 0).

According to section 4, dist:0 should result in the last distance being used, which defaults to 4 (without updating the distance ring buffer; not that it matters).

Thus, this is really a repeated sequence of (length:5, dist:4) commands.

For the first command, there is no output, so it falls under the static dictionary case according to:
```
if distance is less than the max allowed distance plus one
	move backwards distance bytes in the uncompressed data,
	and copy CLEN bytes from this position to
	the uncompressed stream
else
	look up the static dictionary word, transform the word as
	directed, and copy the result to the uncompressed stream
```
This copies "world" to the output.

For all subsequent commands, there is sufficient data in the output such that a distance of 4 is always satisfied by the dynamic dictionary. Thus, copying "orldo" the first time, "rldor" the second time, "ldorl" the third time, etc.

> That makes sense. Ideally I think that would be configurable as well, e.g.: lower_percentile and upper_percentile.

For now it's possible to choose which quantile to use, eventually we can add the percentile option if the quantile configuration is not flexible enough.

> Also, seems like sub-sampling doesn't quite do what we want: ideally we wouldn't exit the positions but hold them for the whole week. I suppose one would need to have the same signal for all days in the week to achieve that.

The portfolio is holding the positions for 1 day because the code calls `create_pyfolio_input(period='1D', ... )`. If we switched `period` to '5D', which is one of the periods computed by `get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns`, the position would be held for 5 days. The reason I used '1D'  is I didn't find a good example to use the 5 days period.  I didn't want to give the misleading idea that there is a good reason to trade a 5 days signal every 5 days.

I believe that rebalancing every 5 days is not the best way of trading a 5 days signal. A better way to do that would be to trade 1/5 th of the portfolio every subsequent day and rebalance each 1/5th portfolio every 5 days. This would result in the same transaction cost, but the slippage impact would be 1/5th, the portfolio capacity would be 5 times bigger, the volatility of the portfolio would be lower, the factor would be traded every single day making it more statistically robust and independent of the starting day.

Deleted my prior comment. @dylanb - as an issue description, I think you know it should have better information in it so that action could be taken @davegandy - The claim made in the footer of the http://fontawesome.io/ website which says " Screen Reader Compatible" is demonstrably false. Given the icon fa-bed (http://fontawesome.io/icon/bed/) Using the following markup: It creates CSS generated content: "\f236"; There are three accessibility problems that may result: 1. (and this is @dylanb's point) Screen readers *may* read that CSS generated content. The workaround for this scenario is to use aria-hidden like so: which would allow a screen reader to ignore it. BUT this raises another concern 2. Because it creates generated content, there's no text alternative. Imagine I did this That has no content in the text node of the DOM. It is, in almost all cases, a blank link. Going back to the above, users *may* get access to the generated content which, in all likelihood, actually sucks to hear on a screen reader. So here's the fix Github That uses the 'sr-only' class naming convention from Bootstrap, BTW. That's not necessary, but what is necessary is basically that (if you don't want the word "Github" to appear) the span be positioned off screen to hide it. This is effectively the same as having an alt attribute on an image But wait! There's more 3) There's a final problem: Windows High Contrast Mode. With Windows High Contrast mode turned on, the font icon is essentially rendered invisible. The problem is that it even with the offscreen span approach, the text in the span won't be visible. In short, to be fully accessible something else has to happen - namely detecting HCM and doing something to show the previously-hidden span: http://jsfiddle.net/karlgroves/XR8Su/6/ TL;DR: @dylanb is right. Your claim of being "Screen Reader Compatible" is inaccurate. I recommend removing this claim and improving documentation so people understand the accessibility implications of icon fonts.

Originally there were replication username and password in the connection string in DCS, but we decided to get rid from them in order to be secure.

DCS can be accessed by different application and users and they are able to read any keys from there.
We are against giving superuser access to everybody. Having of replication credentials is nearly the same as having superuser, because it gives you permission to fetch all database files...
Sure, in some cases it is possible to configure authentication for DCS, but Patroni doesn't support it yet.
In any case you will have to distribute logins/passwords (and/or certificates) in a secure way, either for DCS or for superuser/replication.

Faites comme vous voulez.
Je dis simplement qu'un test n'a de valeur qu'à partir du moment où il renvoie "vert" ou "rouge" (en fait, il a même une valeur décroissante avec sa stabilité, [cf. 1.5](http://pythontesting.net/strategy/why-most-unit-testing-is-waste/)).
En revanche, maintenir du code a un coût non nul, ne serait-ce que le coût cognitif de sa prise en compte dans une hiérarchie de dossiers. Donc, un test qui n'est pas exécuté est juste un coût.
J'enlève donc systématiquement de mes codebases les tests non exécutés, au même titre que du code mort ou du code mis en commentaire. Maintenant, je ne suis pas mainteneur ici  :wink:

@nguy This sounds like a great addition to Py-ART.

First, the functionality to read in a single field from a file is available in Py-ART but could be improved.  The **field_names** and **exclude_fields** parameters in Py-ART's [`read` functions](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart-docs-travis/user_reference/io.html) can be used to select out a single field variable provided that you know before hand the field names used in the file. That said, for many of the formats supported by Py-ART the current implementation requires reading the entire file even when a single field is specified by these parameters.  As such, the speed-up might be less spectacular then expected. In some of the supported formats reading only a single or a limited number of sweeps might provide a more significant speed up in read time. 

Dropping down a layer of abstraction below the Radar class to the underlying file format classes such as [MdvFile](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart/dev/dev_reference/generated/pyart.io.mdv.MdvFile.html#pyart.io.mdv.MdvFile), [NEXRADLevel2File](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart/dev/dev_reference/generated/pyart.io.nexrad_level2.NEXRADLevel2File.html#pyart.io.nexrad_level2.NEXRADLevel2File) and [SigmetFile](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart/dev/dev_reference/generated/pyart.io._sigmetfile.SigmetFile.html#pyart.io._sigmetfile.SigmetFile) allows for a field and/or sweep specific reading of the data which does not requires additional data access which would speed things up.  Trying to generalize this functionality to all supported file formats through the Radar object might be challenging for a number of reasons, including that some formats interleave field and sweep data making accessing a single variable difficult.  Still given sufficient time I think this could be accomplished.  

For an implementation of this proposal I would suggest adding a **only_fields** parameter to `pyart.io.read` and underlying `read_` functions which contain a list of fields to read exclusively as well as a **nsweeps** parameter which could be either a integer specifying that the first nsweeps should be read, or a list containing the sweep number from the file to read.

I don't think using `Database.snapshot` solves the problem.  Using the current methods:

```python
results = database.execute_sql(SQL)  # returns StreamedResultSet
```

versus using a snapshot:

```python
with database.snapshot() as snapshot:
    results = snapshot.execute_sql(SQL) # returns StreamedResultSet
```

The  'StreamedResultSet' we return wraps a low-level GAPIC entity with no existing / obvious link to our session.  Given that the user may not iterate to the end (or at all), we would likely have to had some machinery for a "deferred return to pool" and trigger that from `StreamedResultSet.__del__`.

The other option would be to require that user applications complete all iteration over the `StreamedResultSet` inside the `with database.snapshot() as snapshot:` block.

The Protobuf representation of set() and the update() example that Sam provided is the same (and the one you provided in the update.pb example). The main difference is that set() takes a literal object value and that we extract the field paths from it, whereas update takes a list of field path/value pairs.

When a user calls `set(..., merge)`, we only transmit the data that is being merged and perform an update with an automatically obtained field mask.

This is a fun one. Numpy's scalar type system generates a bunch of types automatically, distinguishing for example between ULONG and ULONGLONG - which are the same on 64-bit systems, and end up with the same name but different type objects:

```
>>> from numpy import *
>>> uint64
<type 'numpy.uint64'>
>>> id(uint64)
3212084976704
>>> ulonglong
<type 'numpy.uint64'>
>>> id(ulonglong)
3212084976256
```

These then also get dragged into all of the Numpy type conversion apparatus, like `numpy.cast`.

One thing I've noticed with this PR that the wheels it picks aren't don't always match the ABI tags they should. At first I thought this was just when using the `--platform` tag on OS X, but even without `--platform` I've found that on Linux I get some `cp27m` wheels and some `cp27mu` wheels in the same archive. I think maybe we'll need to add the ability to specify an ABI tag when using `--platform` and we should use `pip.pep425tags.get_abi_tag()` to determine the correct ABI tag without it.

there's also no exclude syntax that I'm aware of for python requirements, tho that'd be nice in cases like this and could be worth pursuing.

beyond that, it seems like the only way to make this particular package cross-platform-resolver friendly would be to drop the env marker extras in favor of making users explicitly define those (e.g. `doit[linux]` vs `doit[osx]`) - but that's likely not a generally acceptable solution.

an inversion of that idea that may be a viable escape hatch in the longer term on a per-platform axis tho, could be to implement a `pex --intransitive` mode that would force you to explicitly specify the transitive set of requirements. this would not solve for the "need MacFSEvents only for OSX && pyinotify only for Linux" problem tho because there's no way to specify e.g. a combination of (requirement, platform) as specification.

so yeah, a harder problem than it appears on the surface.

dropping into a repl has been the default for `pex` when not passed `-o` for as long as I can remember:

```
$ pex pex==0.7.0 -c pex -o /tmp/pex
$ /tmp/pex
Python 2.7.10 (default, Jul 14 2015, 19:46:27) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 6.0 (clang-600.0.39)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
(InteractiveConsole)
>>> ^D
```

and I believe that it's the normal pex environment isolation you're seeing here that is inherently not providing for access to requests when the repl entrypoint is invoked:

```
$ sudo pip2.7 install requests --upgrade
Requirement already up-to-date: requests in /Library/Python/2.7/site-packages
Cleaning up...
$ python2.7 -c 'import requests; print requests.__version__'
2.7.0
$ pex
Python 2.7.10 (default, Jul 14 2015, 19:46:27) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 6.0 (clang-600.0.39)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
(InteractiveConsole)
>>> import requests
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named requests
>>> ^D
```

so afaict the behavior seen here is as expected. the execution of the pex console script passed via `-c` (now the main entrypoint for pex.pex) is just dropping you into an _isolated_ repl because you've provided it with no dependency inputs for the new execution context.

A few notes after additional reading:
* The binary builder shards already push whls for pants itself: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/042a81705ff237f0cc57d09de5f54079690abe8c/.travis.yml#L423-L425 ...but they do it in a relatively risky way:
    * the whls are always pushed for the current `src/python/pants/version.py` value, meaning that many pushes are overwrites
    * a release consuming the whls will just consume whatever the most recent pushed binaries for that version happen to be: if you're running a dev release from master, this might be some sha a few beyond where the release notes landed

So, adjustments to the plan:

3. Reuse the travis `whl` building path, but adjust it to:
    * namespace the whls under the current SHA, to avoid collisions [0]
    * push SHA-suffixed releases for branches
    * push un-suffixed releases for tags
4. To avoid the release race condition above, I'll adjust the release workflow to move the tag push much earlier in the process. Because it will be a tag, CI will build un-suffixed whls for the version, which the release script can consume.

[0] Both a suffixed and un-suffixed version will be built for a particular SHA if it both exists in a branch and is tagged, or is tagged multiple times, but these collisions should be harmless because the packages are identical.

A quick update.  I have done rather more than I intended!

* Updated the `conf.py` file, and enabled all sorts of useful goodies. The default role is set to `any` (see above`, which allows much easier and less verbose cross-referencing.  Better use of `inter sphinx` for x-refs to the python docs, and to the requests library.
* The auto generated stub files have been reorganised into an api subdirectory which makes them more manageable. And they have been stripped down to a couple of lines each, by sensible setting of defaults (which can be overridden on a case-by-case basis if necessary)
* Fixed all sorts of bugs relating to the inheritance diagram (which involved learning more about graphviz than I cared to), which did not work well with the RTD theme. We now have a javascript fix in place, proper colours, readable fonts, and the diagram looks **much** nicer (and clicking on it works again)
* A lot of the docstrings have been updated (I will continue with this) for napoleon, and tidied up for clarity, spellings, etc.
* There is now a shortcut for Github PRs and bugs. ``:issue:`123` `` now cross-links to issue 123 on Github and generates link text like this: [#123](https://github.com/SoCo/SoCo/issues/123).  ``:PR:`456` `` does the same for PRs.  Generation of release notes should now be easier.
* There is some custom tempting to make RTD respect our choice of pigment highlighting style. I have left it as the default Sphinx style for the moment.

I had been using the music_service module to obtain the following information from my Sonos system, which I'd thought was needed to add Spotify tracks or containers:

service_id (or sid)
account_serial_number (or sn)
desc

These details are used as follows (replacing them with XXXXXX in this example):

<EnqueuedURI>x-sonos-spotify:spotify%3atrack%3a3rhINhxUFrwB8vw7fWAVC4?sid=XXXXXX&flags=8224&sn=XXXXXX</EnqueuedURI>
<EnqueuedURIMetaData><DIDL-Lite xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/" xmlns:upnp="urn:schemas-upnp-org:metadata-1-0/upnp/" xmlns:r="urn:schemas-rinconnetworks-com:metadata-1-0/" xmlns="urn:schemas-upnp-org:metadata-1-0/DIDL-Lite/"><item id="10032020spotify%3atrack%3a3rhINhxUFrwB8vw7fWAVC4" parentID="1004206cspotify%3aalbum%3a4onPyHor2yOlVxCsIaGyHH" restricted="true"><dc:title>Humans</dc:title><upnp:class>object.item.audioItem.musicTrack</upnp:class><desc id="cdudn" nameSpace="urn:schemas-rinconnetworks-com:metadata-1-0/">XXXXXX</desc></item></DIDL-Lite></EnqueuedURIMetaData>

Now I'm wondering whether any of this information is necessary. Having looked at the node-sonos project, sid and sn can seeming be replaced randomly with single digit numerals, but this needs further research.

Also from the node-sonos project, desc seems to be in the form:

'SA_RINCON' + region + '_X_#Svc' + region + '-0-Token'

where region is 3079 for the US or 2311 for the EU. Again, more testing is needed. If anyone else has more info about this, I'd be interested to hear it.

> Why is that needed?

To aid in debugging.

We recently received some application logs submitted by our users with behavior I couldn't explain or reproduce. The root cause was that our build system had used the wrong version of hidapi and we hadn't noticed before we distributed. Because it took such a long time to figure out so I resolved to add version numbers for our packages to the application logs so if this type of thing happened again it'd be really obvious. While doing this we found all but two packages we used make this available via a `__version__`  variable or something similar.

> from pkg_resources import get_distribution
> get_distribution('hidapi').version

This works in development but we've run into two problems building this into our distributable app because it relies on setuptools. Our windows build process coped with this ok but it included a lot of dependencies we weren't using before and we couldn't get this to work on osx.

In the end we've reverted to 

```
def get_hidapi_version():
    hid_file, regex = hid.__file__, '(hidapi[^\\\/]*)'
    prog = re.compile(regex, re.IGNORECASE)
    matches = prog.search(hid_file)
    if matches:
        return matches.group(0)
    else:
        return hid_file
```

The `btn` class is not defined by udata but [by the bootstrap framework](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/3.3/css/#buttons) as base for every/any button (including [button sibling an input field](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/3.3/components/#input-groups-buttons)).
The `btn-xs` class is also defined by the bootstrap framework as a [sizing class](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/3.3/css/#buttons-sizes) (with `btn-sm` and `btn-lg`), including in [the case of input groups](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/3.3/components/#input-groups-sizing)
The `btn-default` is also [defined by the bootstrap framework as basic theming/semantic classes](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/3.3/css/#buttons-options)

The other classes are semantic classes (`btn-more`, `btn-follow`...) defined by udata, allowing to add style to the existing bootstrap ones wihtout breaking the entire styling and should be where customization occurs.
Either there is already a custom class which needs to be customized, either you should add one for the case you want to customize.

In every case, you should avoid modifications on the base bootstrap classes unless:
- this is part of component and needed to fix a sife-effect bug => the style reference should be namespaced (`.my-component .btn`)
- the purpose is to apply a common style on the base bootstrap style (ex. set `btn-default` a given color)

**Note**: There might be some places where styling is misapplied on the base bootstrap classes and this cases require to be fixed.

> I am used to being given a secret key or credential through a web interface and then doing something like:

I agree that this mode of usage would be much better for developers and scripting use cases. We unfortunately don't have that capability at present, so, working with what is currently available...

We need to document the shortest path(s) to getting and using credentials for automation.
So I think there's a significant documentation improvement to do ( e.g. #256 if I can ever get the time), but I can also try to answer your issue to get you up an running here. Maybe if this answer is good enough it will be a basis for a section in the docs.

The [SDK tutorial](https://globus-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tutorial/) _is_ more complicated than fetching an API key, Personal Access Token, or other simple, static credential. However, it can be manipulated into producing a similar end result.

The basic flow you want will probably be to create an app, login to it on your laptop or other device with a functioning browser, and then to get the credentials resulting from login + the app's registration info to your cluster or other remote environment.
- You need to use refresh tokens for long term credentials, which can be fetched with a simple option to the login flow, as detailed in [one of the advanced sections of the tutorial](https://globus-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tutorial/#advanced-2-refresh-tokens-never-login-again)
- This can be done by following the SDK Tutorial and then adding the refresh token flag
- Your browser doesn't need to be open on the machine which will receive your refresh token(s), here's how this can work:
  - remote machine without graphical session (e.g. SSH) has client ID info from app registration
  - remote machine generates a login link, per Tutorial content
  - you copy that login link to your browser, on the local machine
  - you do a login flow in your browser, all local, and follow it until it takes you to https://auth.globus.org/v2/web/auth-code
  - you take the resulting Authorization Code and paste it to the remote machine
  - remote machine exchanges the Authorization Code for Refresh Tokens
- Pass the refresh token(s) to [RefreshTokenAuthorizer](https://globus-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/authorization/#globus_sdk.RefreshTokenAuthorizer) objects -- this encodes your Authorization
- Pass said `RefreshTokenAuthorizer` to the client class you want to use, presumably `TransferClient`

You can see an example of this very flow by installing the Globus CLI (`pip install --user globus-cli`) on a remote machine and running `globus login`. Trying this out to see how it works would be a good step in getting familiar with what the flow looks like.

There's another way of handling credentials which might interest you, which is to use Client Credentials, per [this example](https://globus-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/examples/client_credentials/#using-clientcredentialsauthorizer).
This flow is simpler than the interactive login flow -- it's just exchanging a client ID + client secret for tokens -- but it has the disadvantage that it gives you tokens for the "user" `<client ID>@clients.auth.globus.org`, so you may need to grant that user additional permissions (vs using your own user and its associated permissions).
