I expect this to be a common question, so wanted to add the conversation from chat:
>  How can there be multiple blocks that are new? Because ChainDB.import_block is importing 1 block at a time

It's returning the blocks that are new to the canonical chain. Let's say you have chain:

```
A -- B - C
  `- D
```

where C is the current canonical head.

Then you import E with parent D, where the total difficulty of E is higher than C. Now, you have added D and E to the canonical chain, and removed B and C.

Great. I think we need to get this out, as I think it's causing the problems with #2181 as well, because the internal metadata for Anaconda 2.5.0 is inconsistent, and cannot be satisfied. In particular:

- `pickleshare-0.5-py35_0.tar.bz2` requires `path.py 7.6.1` internally, but Anaconda 2.5 ships with `path.py 8.1.2`. The repodata.json file fixes this by relaxing the requirement to simply `path.py`
- `python-3.5.1-0.tar.bz2` requires `openssl 1.0.2d` internally, but Anaconda 2.5 ships with `openssl 1.0.2f`. The repodata.json file relaxes this to `openssl 1.0.2*`.

It is an optional dependency of keras.  Keras supports theano and tensorflow as backends.  Because conda does not have support for optional dependencies right now, the pattern is generally to include all dependencies of interest.  theano is a dependency of our keras package, even if it is only an optional dependency of the upstream keras package.

You should be able to configure Keras to not use the theano backend, and that's a better way than trying to fight the dependency stuff.

Fundamental issue here is that we have to be able to update metadata over time, most commonly to put upper bounds on the versions of dependencies, but there are also times we need to make other patches.

We intend to implement something similar to https://gist.github.com/jjhelmus/bf9489e3847d8e1737afca7d04b2f66d within `conda index`.  Best place to track that is https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/2407.
This is actually the behavior as designed. When channel priority is false, version _ties_ are broken by channel priority.

OK, this is just speculation, but I think I have an idea about what happened.  Re-creating it to confirm would take me close to an hour, which I don't have time to do right now, but here is my hunch: somehow conda got to thinking that both `2.7.10-0` and `2.7.10-1` versions of the `python` package were installed.  The `conda install` command wanted to use the latest version `2.7.10-1` (already installed) and to do so, it identified that `2.7.10-0` (already installed) should be removed.  Since `2.7.10-1` was already installed, it wasn't installed again, but "removing" `2.7.10-0` meant (effectively) deleting a set of files that were, in fact, the `2.7.10-1` files.

If this is the case (TBD by myself or @mmarchetti), how can conda allow the environment get into a state where there are multiple copies of the same package installed?

The problem with constrained-random testbenches is that its not possible to predict which testcase (test+seed combination) will generate a known error message. So, known error messages are rather testbench-specific and not testcase-specific in UVM. So, #293 doesnt help here.

I was able to use the output file stream in my post_check function and parse it for UVM-specific as well as HDL-$error generated error patterns from an "error pattern" file.
I was also able to exclude specific patterns from an "Exclusion rules" pattern file, so that a known-error message (UVM-specific or other) can be excluded and doesnt contribute to a FAIL decision.
Whenever there is a $error message triggered, the testcase always quits with a FAIL due to the "onerror quit", without taking my "error exclusion" rules into account.

**Update:** There's a problem with setting indices to read-only (at least in Elasticsearch 1.6 and using the elasticsearch python module):

### Create index
```python
>>> client.indices.create(index=“foo”)
{u’acknowledged’: True}
```

### Index a document
```python
>>> client.index(index=“foo”, doc_type=“mytype”, body={“name”:”James”})
{u’_type’: u’mytype’, u’_id’: u’AU345CoLYzProjwnt2EQ’, u’created’: True, u’_version’: 1, u’_index’: u’foo’}
```

### Set index to read-only
```python
>>> client.indices.put_settings(index=“foo”, body=‘blocks.write=True’)
{u’acknowledged’: True}
```

I had this same problem and in my case the issue was that pip installed `azure-cli-ml` to a location that wasn't looked at when executing the system default `python`.

You could either fix the python / pip installation so that both point to the same location or check which python pip uses and then explicitly load the module with that python binary. For example:

```
/usr/local/opt/python/bin/python2.7 -m azure.cli ml account -h
```

You can see how `az` loads the module with something like `cat $(which az)`.

I also noticed that just doing `pip install azure-cli-ml` might result into version compatibility issues. Getting tested versions can be done with:

```
pip install -r https://aka.ms/az-ml-o16n-cli-requirements-file
```

@nitzmahone That's the behavior I wanted: api_version as a default value less important than profile. For instance, if you write:
```python
print(c.resource_skus.api_version)
```
'2016-04-30-preview' does not exists for this one, so I have to use the profile, so the idea was:
- Use profile first
- If no key in profile, fallback to api_version
- If still nothing, fail

This indeed means that if you don't change the profile, changing api_version is a partial change. It's why I updated the doc:
```python
    :param str api_version: API version to use if no profile is provided, or if
     missing in profile.
```

If you didn't find it obvious, I'm a little concerned, I expect SDK to be obvious so you don't ask yourself this kind of question.

* Get tags for a file based on its interpreter name (read from the shebang)

@andymo The simplest fix is to just wrap your calls in a Task, like so:

```python
async def get(self):
    session = aiobotocore.get_session()
    client = session.create_client('s3')

    task = asyncio.ensure_future(client.list_buckets())
    response = await task

    self.write(response)
```

I made a magic for this exact purpose, I'm calling it ``jdc`` - or Jupyter dynamic classes.  It's super simple, but basically it allows you to type

```python
%%add_to our_class
def our_method(self, our_variable):
    print our_variable
```
and that method will be added to the class ``our_class`` (and all objects of that type) with access to self.  You can also add it to only a single object with

```python
%%add_to our_object
def our_method(self, our_variable):
    print our_variable
```

Documentation is at [https://alexhagen.github.io/jdc](https://alexhagen.github.io/jdc).  I hope this helps

> In other words, it's setup.py install and setup.py test that are obsolete

Can you point out to any evidence of this? You sound pretty adamant about this but I'm not sure it's true.

* wheels can't replace source distributions and "setup.py install" entirely. In case of packages with binary extensions (i.e. not universal wheels), a package author can't upload a wheel distribution for all possible platforms Python is supported on (not to mention that for "linux", wheels don't differentiate between different linux distros and that can be a problem). This is why source distributions are still required. sdists (installed with setup.py install) are here to stay, alongside wheels.
* easy_install is still actively maintained and widely used. Maybe everyone should switch to pip, but not everyone has.
* It's not just easy_install that uses sdists and 'setup.py install' for installation. There's also buildout, conda (when building packages), pytest (as seen in this ticket), and probably many more tools that cropped up over the years.

The packages you mentioned: Django, Flask, SQLAlchemy, even pip and setuptools all have source distributions. I would like to ask that you please don't drop sdists, otherwise we will not be able to use schemtics (we use buildout).

One of the reasons we removed `PERU_FILE_NAME` was that, by encouraging users to set it globally, we were making it more likely you'd break other projects in a confusing way. For scripts and Makefiles, I think `--peru-file` and `--sync-dir` are better options. And for interactive use, you could define an alias that used those flags. What you'd miss out on is the ability to call peru from subdirectories of your project, and still have it find your YAML file. If that's something you were missing, I could see adding back in a `--peru-search-name` flag or something like that, again with the goal of letting you define an alias. But that global `PERU_FILE_NAME` setting scares me.

@andersruneson I do use admin/manager account to bind to the ldap server. my synapse is set up properly. The problem is that during the phase of fetching metadata for the account creation (line 168), this search is done by the user that has been created. 
``` 
search_base=self.ldap_base,
                    search_filter=query,
                    attributes=[
                        self.ldap_attributes['name'],
                        self.ldap_attributes['mail']
```
The problem is that my olcAccess prevents users from searching the entire `search_base` but only themselfs. The query above will exit with no results as the user cant query that directory. So we had to modify the query as posted by @antil0pa 
search_base="cn={ldap_user},{ldap_base}".format(
                         ldap_user=localpart,
                         ldap_base=self.ldap_base
                     ),
```
So only then users can lookup the metadata information as the search base points to their account directly. This is probably not the prettiest way to do that, but we host multiple services using ldap authentication, and they all work well except for synapse.
I think that metadata search should be done by admin/manager account and not the user that's being authenticated.

An option to maintain the case sensitivity would be a good one to add.  The issue we experienced was that for some [insane] reason (presumably to support Windows as a client platform), Teradata server can maintain case sensitivity while operating against namespaces with case insensitivity.  The decision to make a "safe" name for accessing this in Python was driven by the possibility (and, in our experience, reality) that people would create tables/columns with seemingly arbitrary casing and that this would break down interactions with these objects via giraffez.  With all the names changed predictably we thought it would help to address this use case.  In the situation it would not, it definitely makes sense to add an option to maintain capitalization.  My naive implementation would say that setting the operating mode for case sensitivity should be set at the package/module level (and therefore apply globally for that Python interpreter), but I'm open to other ideas/submissions on how to solve this issue.

@simonklb thanks for putting that repo together.  I do see the problem that you are trying to solve, but I'm not sure that dependencies are the way to go.  But that could be because I'm not understanding the code that you've written.  By example:

```python
installed = Flag("installed")
configured = Flag("configured", requires={installed})
started = Flag("started", requires={configured})
available = Flag("available", requires={started})
```

This, I'm guessing, defines 4 flags with an increasing set of dependencies.

However, this piece of code confuses me:
```python
@handler(
    when(installed),
    when_not(configured)
)
def configure():
    print("CONFIGURE")
    Bus.set_flag(configured)
```
It confuses me because this looks like a slightly re-written form of the current system; but I can't see where the 'dependency' bit comes in.  i.e. I thought the handler specification might have looked like this:

```python
@handler(when_not(configured))
def configure():
    print("CONFIGURE")
    Bus.set_flag(configured)
```
i.e. configured being set implies installed being set, as it is dependent on it being set and _couldn't be otherwise_?  I'm not sure about that last bit in _italics_.

I also have some nagging questions such as: 

1. If you clear the installed flag, does that automatically clear the dependent flags?
2. What if you wanted an inverse dependency?
3. Wouldn't this start to hide _what_ the flags are ?

As an alternative, I'll put down some initial thoughts I've had that _might_ be trying to solve the same set of problems for you (and others) to pick apart:

I'll start with a question:

## Q: what if you could _compose_ flags?

_This is my view; please feel free to argue/correct as appropriate!_

I started my career in embedded systems design, and at the time, state machines were all the rage.  And the way we organised those in C was to use a matrix of pointers to functions (yes, really!) that was (usually) indexed by column (event) and row (state).  The theory is/was is that the system was decomposed into a number of states and actions that could happen were the events.  When an event occurred, the function to run was determined by the (state, event) lookup, which was either a function or a null(0), which means that nothing happened for that event, in that state.

Functions could change the overall (global) state variable which means a future identical event would trigger a different function.  And this is how many protocol handlers were written.

charms.reactrive has echos of this, except that the 'events' that change the system are _hidden_ in the interfaces (as they come, mutate and go), and potentially on the unit itself (in terms of files changing or other detectable changes).  Also, rather than having a single 'global' state, the state of the charm is encoded in multiple flags, that taken _together_ give the overall state of the charm at any point in time.  The difference, of course, is an _event_ isn't explicitly used to derive the next function to run, rather the _event_ may trigger a cascade of functions to run as the global state mutates via the changes to the flags/states in the system.

Interestingly, the closest thing we have to an _event_ is a hook, and one proposal is to remove them _entirely_ from reactive2 such that events are completely hidden.  The individual/groups of flags associated with a set of events then becomes a _proxy_ for those events. An example are the `{relation-name}.connected` and `{relation-name}.available` states/flags that have become the convention of many interfaces.  The states (roughly) correspond with:

* `{relation-name}.connected` - there's something at the other end of a relation.
* `{relation-name}.available` - the data available on the interface object's methods is _valid_ in some way determined by that interface method.

Because the concept of an event such as 'the data on the interface has changed' has been filtered out from the interface (after all the hook exists, it's just not proxied to a flag), charm layer code needs to always run the associated handler with `{relation-name}.available` and idempotently _do-the-right-thing_ if the data has changed.

I think the later part is probably a strength of the framework; handlers determine whether things need to be done, and the flags just 'memoise' that a particular condition exists without the charm having to discover it.  This probably means we need _fewer_ flags, rather than more, if code is to work _with_ the design outcomes of the framework; although it seems inefficient to repeatedly run the same set of handlers when nothing has potentially changed.

---

## A: Composable conditions for handlers

Even if we embrace _fewer_ flags, I still think there is some merit in being able to express combinations of flags in a more comprehensible form.  And for this, I would proposal _composable conditions_.

e.g. if we have:

```python
@when('installed')
@when_not('configured')
def do_configuration():
    pass
```

Why not instead have:

```python
@condition('installed & !configured')
def do_configuration():
    pass
```

If it also needed an interface to be available:

```python
@condition('installed & db.available & !configured')
def do_configuration():
    pass
```

Note, I'm not hung up on the syntax; I am very interested in being able to use logic to combine flags beyond the simple when() AND when_not AND ....  I'd like to through some ORs in there, although I know you can express OR via AND using De Morgans ... it just looks messy, and requires multiple functions to achieve the same result.

---

So, to summarise, I may have completely understood flag dependencies; I do agree that we need to work through the issues and deficiencies that the current implementation has, and work out how to keep compatibility with the current system.  I've proposed another concept to deal with complex sets of `when`, `when_not` and other decorators on a handler.

Thoughts and comments welcome!

There is no rule that says "extracting common functionality into a function is only warranted if the functionality involves more than X lines of code." By your logic, we wouldn't even have introduced `require`, a two liner.  So, yes I want it. With a dedicated function, the error message becomes consistent and includes the path to the problematic file, which is something people will inherently forget to do. It also funnels every case where an invalid BAM constitutes an error through a single function, allowing for future improvements to that function.

I agree with **both** of you! IMO, let's ignore redefinitions of `_` *unless it was imported (including imported `as`).

Rationale: `_` is a common throwaway variable. Doing three consecutive for-loops in Python 2 using the `_` name should not raise warnings. However, you never import a thing under a name if you don't intend to use it somehow. In this case, if anything overwrites our imported name (including by a for-loop or other tuple-unpacking throwaway variable), it should definitely raise a warning.

A few comments/questions:

1. Are you willing to install the runtime as a pre-requisite or do you want to ship the runtime with the application? There is a feature that is being designed to enable apps that depend on a runtime still having an exe. This world would allow your app to be much smaller but would require that .NET Core Runtime that you depend on be installed on the target machine.

2. We have a preview of a linker that will shake out code that is not used and usually makes the app smaller but doesn't change the number of dll's. Check out https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/samples/linker-instructions.md

3. The next step after the linker will be something that is being called "bundling" which is exactly what you talked about. The ability to have a single executable artifact. There isn't a clear timeline/roadmap on that one but I know there are people looking into it.

4. You mentioned users not wanting to integrate such large numbers of files. Is the option of putting up a zip file that you just tell people to extract an option? If all the files were in a sub folder and when they opened your app it was just an exe (maybe also a config) and then a sub folder with "dependencies", would that help?

With regard to #1376, the behavior @bmw describes applies when a totally new cert is created (that doesn't overlap with existing names), but not when a cert is renewed (by overlapping with the names in an existing cert). There is logic in `client.py` related to the lineage naming of new certs

https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt/client.py#L301

-- here the `domains[0]` selects the first name in the list as the base name for the certificate lineage -- and in `cli.py` for treating identical cert requests as renewals regardless of the order in which the domains are mentioned

https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt/cli.py#L208

-- here `set()` ignores the order in which the certs were specified to the client when comparing them against each previously-obtained cert.

And yes, I'm not at all sure that a user would anticipate this behavior on the basis of our current documentation.

> The question then is still what is the meaning of 1m? I think falling back on the meaning defined by strftime is ok, not sure what other conventions there are that are context-free.

The problem with this is that the `m` in strftime is actually *replaced by values*, not used as an indicator of what the value it just printed *was*, so it's a convention for formatting strings, not a convention for string formats (generally string formats include this sort of thing positionally).

I think it's probably worth finding examples of people actually formatting these things to see how we can best get the "most likely right thing", and design the parser ahead of time such that it's not amazingly complicated to specify the context.

I'm actually less worried about the `1m` situation and more worried about the `1y1m` situation. If I saw `1y1m`, I'd think that means "1 year, 1 month", but it could also mean "1 year, 1 minute", and it only becomes ambiguous when it becomes either "1y1m1w" or "1y1d1m".

> > @bgruening Yep, in case of ambiguity or unparsability the ID field can be used.

> So why not have a more explicit annotation in the first place. I'm still a fan of explicit over implicit and CWL is already so verbose that this probably does not matter, isn't it?

I agree that explicit is better, and I bet that specifying a tool ID will be apart of various best practice guides, `planemo lint`, etcetera :-)

> So if I write such a script to call bowtie-build foo; bowtie bar; magic.py baz I need to create a separate conda package instead of simply specifying bowite=2.2 and python-magic=0.1? This would be possible, conda supports meta-packages but means more work.

Right, CWL doesn't aim to solve tool delivery. Either the program is already installed, the user installs it themselves (and tells the CWL implementation about it by specifying `cwl:requirements: EnvVarRequirement: { PATH: /home/username/bin/ }` in the input job object) or the CWL implementation uses the information available (`baseCommand`, `id`, `with`, `DockerRequirement`) to setup a suitable runtime environment.

Here is a summary of my current thinking. Thank you @bgruening @chapmanb @jmchilton for challenging me :-)

1) Use the existing `id` field to specify tool identity using bio.tools, or another namespace. Platforms can then use those IDs to install a conda package, setup a new docker image, et cetera. Note: if bio.tools URLs don't contain the information you need please talk to me and them: they are quite happy to add extra fields! Alternatively we can annotate out packages with the unique IDs. I imagine that there will be a bit of both.

2) Add a `with` field to the CommandLineTool and Workflow CWL spec for tools & libraries that aren't strictly implied by the `id` field.

3) Add `ToolVersionRequirements` to the CWL spec for use in job input documents and elsewhere. Behavior beyond pure string matching of version numbers is undefined.

Figured it out: We have to replace zero values with very small numbers before dividing:

```
item_norms[item_norms == 0] = 1e-10
```

Here the full code again:

```
def similar_items(item_id, item_features, model, N=10):
    (item_biased, item_representations) = model.get_item_representations(features=item_features)
    # Cosine similarity
    scores = item_representations.dot(item_representations[item_id])
    item_norms = numpy.linalg.norm(item_representations, axis=1)
    item_norms[item_norms == 0] = 1e-10    
    scores /= item_norms
    best = numpy.argpartition(scores, -N)[-N:]
    return sorted(zip(best, scores[best] / item_norms[item_id]),
                  key=lambda x: -x[1])
```

I'm all for being helpful with timezones. This was my motivation for adding a ``tz`` attribute to the ``Location`` class. The ``solarposition`` module (with some functions in ``tools``) has an attempt at using tz knowledge from either the ``DatetimeIndex`` or the ``Location`` object. Do these fit into your scope or do you want to do something different? I did think that it would be nice if the ``Location`` constructor could use a web service to determine a IANA timezone from a lat/lon. (Or maybe even a lat/lon/tz from a city/state/country search, but that's another topic.)

Guaranteeing that time zones are handled correctly is hard unless you're very restrictive about it. My inclination is to leave the vast majority of the work to pandas. All localized times in pandas are stored as utc internally, so we can and should take advantage of that (as the description above does). 

We could require that all times be localized, or we could throw warnings whenever we detect that people are using non-localized times.

Concerning solar time... I just rely on the zenith and azimuth calculations for a given lat/lon. Is there an application for solar time other than for the actual calculation of those quantities of interest? I don't know how pandas would reasonably incorporate something that requires a specific lat/lon. Maybe we're talking about two different quantities? I think of solar time as the local time shifted to make the minimum zenith occur at 12:00 noon.

I disagree.  The SAPM function fits a polynomial without a constraint that f2(AOI)<=1.  It’s a bad choice for an empirical function.  This is a sore point with me, because physically, you shouldn’t increase POA if you move away from normal (AOI=0), and by definition f2(0) = 1.  We’ve revisited our outdoor measurement procedures and determined that the ‘measurements’ (in quotes because you compute values of f2(AOI) from measured Isc and irradiance) of f2(AOI) which slightly exceeded 1 were erroneous, for several reasons.  Bruce King had a paper at PVSC 42(?) on this subject.

Also, the function should either take the absolute value of aoi or return nan for aoi less than 0.
I’d return nan and a warning if AOI<0, that’s a problem with the inverse cosine implementation.  By definition you should get 0<=AOI<180.  AOI>90 indicates that the sun in behind the panel.

Yes, `seed` and `_seed` are different. While `seed` is the original seed from the configuration, `_seed` is another value generated (deterministically) from that original seed. While `seed` is the same everywhere, `_seed` will be [different every time the function is called](http://sacred.readthedocs.io/en/latest/randomness.html#special-arguments). But it depends on the global seed and thus the experiment should still be reproducible. 

The `_seed` and `_rnd` is a feature I went a bit overboard with. It is meant to support a function that should be called several times in an experiment and produce different random behavior everytime. But I wanted to make sure that it was still reproducible, and even better: that its randomness would only depend on  its own call history. So if you have this:

``` python
@ex.capture
def testA(_seed):
    print('A', _seed)

@ex.capture
def testB(_seed):
    print('B', _seed)
```

Then calling `testA` several times will give you different values even with a fixed seed: 
```
>>> ./test_randomness.py with seed=42
A 904388214
A 36157956
B 767258318
B 951190362
```
And no matter how you re-arrange the calls of `testA` and `testB` you should get the same:
```
>>> ./test_randomness2.py with seed=42
B 767258318
A 904388214
B 951190362
A 36157956
```
This was done to keep an experiment as reproducible as possible even through code changes, since it localizes the effect of using the random-number generator. But as I said: I think I went a bit overboard with this. :-)

This is a recurring feedback. There is no API method for retrieving elements in a sequence. The background is that the sequence/string functions are derived from a standards draft for SMT-LIB and it does not have a type for characters. In Z3 we can create a unit sequence and do have a type for characters (It is set to 8-bits, other widths can be accomplished using sequences over other bit-widths).

You can emulate a function that takes the nth' element of a list by adding the following for every occurrence of str.at(s, j):

      str.at(s,j) != "" => seq.unit(str.nth(s,j)) = str.at(s,j)

E.g., if str.at returns non-empty string, then the nth element at position j is that element.

I mean... there's a real fundamental difference between "a sequence of bytes" and "a sequence of unicode codepoints". pretty much everything we do in angr is with a sequence of bytes. The thing is that python 3 has changed the `str` class from being a sequence of bytes to being a sequence of unicode codepoints - the `bytes` class is meant to do that now. In python 2 `bytes` is a synonym for `str` in order to ease the transition.

import xmltodict
import base64
import hashlib
from Crypto.Cipher import AES

def parse_refund_result(xml):
    BS = AES.block_size
    pad = lambda s: s + (BS - len(s) % BS) * chr(BS - len(s) % BS)
    unpad = lambda s: s[0:-ord(s[-1])]

    data = xmltodict.parse(xml)['xml']
    req_info = data['req_info'].encode()
    req_info = base64.decodebytes(req_info)
    hash = hashlib.md5()
    hash.update(current_app.config['WECHAT_PAY_API_KEY'].encode())
    key = hash.hexdigest().encode()

    cipher = AES.new(key, AES.MODE_ECB)
    decrypt_bytes = cipher.decrypt(req_info)
    decrypt_str = decrypt_bytes.decode()
    decrypt_str = unpad(decrypt_str)

    info = xmltodict.parse(decrypt_str)['root']
    data = dict(data, **info)
    data.pop('req_info')
    return data

@sigmavirus24 This PR is specifically about the "target" field in a request (basically the request path, the thing that comes after `GET` on the request line). So it's only ever generated by the client and parsed by the server.

Headers are a bit different: for headers, the spec actually requires *allowing* bytes outside the ascii range and only forbids the ascii "control characters" (stuff like NUL and DEL and form-feed). And then as we discovered in #57, browsers actually allow control characters too, and popular software relies on this, so h11 ended up only forbidding NUL and exotic whitespace (#68). So utf8 in `Location:` or JSON values is fine.

The cases where this PR could cause a problem are:

* writing a server that needs to interoperate with a client that sends invalid binary characters (like utf-8) in the target field, without doing proper %-encoding

* writing a client that needs to interoperate with a server that insists on getting raw binary characters (like utf8) in the target field, and won't accept the proper %-encoded equivalent

It's entirely possible that something like this will come up of course, because, well, software.

Does requests provide a way to disable %-encoding and pass through invalid characters as part of the target? Have people asked for such a feature?

@kersommoura, feel free to chime in. Your opinion is important, especially on matters that have a long term impact on Pulp QE.

> One question I have is: does Pulp QE want to maintain smash tests for all plugins? With Pulp 3 I think we'll see more community-contributed plugins like pulp_gem. If plugins start writing smash tests with our current model, PulpQE is going to need to review these PRs, maintain their code, etc. Seems like a better model would be to put the onus on plugins to manage their own smash tests.

I think it's valuable for Pulp QE to be able to write tests for any of the plugins for Pulp 3. However, Pulp QE is a small team, and it seems highly unlikely that we'll be able to thorougly test all plugins. This is especially true if a greater variety of plugins crops up for Pulp 3 than is currently the case for Pulp 2. I see great value in designing a mechanism by which third parties can write integration tests without having to re-invent the wheel, and without the frustration of merging all of those tests into Pulp Smash proper. The bar for contributions is set high, and I'd like to keep that bar high, despite the drawbacks of doing so.

> What if in this case we install two copies of pulp_ansible—one on each host?

This seems like a viable solution. It does mean a little bit of coordination, where both the Pulp 3 installation and the Pulp Smash installation need to be kept in sync. But the burden seems manageable, and it allows Pulp 3 and Pulp Smash to be installed on separate hosts.

> > The Pulp 3 installer (both from-source and from-PyPI) install Pulp and its plugins into non-standard paths.
>
> Yea, we would need to deal with this.

If two copies of pulp_ansible are installed as outlined above, this should no longer be an issue. Cool.

> Doesn't Pulp Smash already namespace plugin tests? Also, I think this responsibility would fall on plugins if they want to write pulp-smash tests so not sure if it's something QE needs to worry about?

Like any Python application, Pulp Smash uses namespaces. However, it does not support [namespace packages](https://packaging.python.org/guides/packaging-namespace-packages/). Explicit work needs to be done to enable namespace packages.

I do think that Pulp QE needs to worry about namespace conflicts. Imagine the following scenario:

1. The pulp_ansible plugin implements a test case called `pulp_smash.tests.pulp3.ansible.api_v3.test_role.RoleTestCase`.
2. Pulp QE writes a test case at the same path.

Whoops. Also, I don't want third-party test writers to have to know about every going-on in Pulp Smash. That seems like an unnecessary burden. Thankfully, the design of namespace packages naturally encourages namespace separation, so this shouldn't be an issue.

> What about a semantically versioned API? This might be a good compromise between stability and the freedom for pulp-smash to innovate/improve.

This is doable. There are projects that both have regular releases and maintain good semantic versioning. As an example, see [Syncthing releases](https://github.com/syncthing/syncthing/releases), which come out on a weekly basis. Some technical debt needs to be paid off, though. Some thoughts that come to mind:

* The `BaseAPITestCase` should be deleted.
* Much code should be moved out of `pulp_smash.*` into `pulp_smash.tests.pulp2.*` and `pulp_smash.tests.pulp3.*`.
* It'd probably be good to go through all constant names with a fine-toothed comb and assign a good naming scheme.

If code is to get out the door sooner rather than later, we can create a mechanism for plug-in tests, but avoid an X release until this is done.

> > Splitting code across repositories means that the linters and unit tests built in to Pulp Smash won't be able to work their magic on the separated code.
>
> In our PRs, we install pulp-smash into Travis and we could certainly run any linters or unit tests on any code changes to the pulp-smash tests inside pulp_ansible or whatever plugin. The reverse (adding new linters, etc) in pulp-smash could be a pain though.

Perhaps the solution here is to say "different projects have different standards." To hold every plugin to the same standard could easily be problematic. Could https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/commit/1e9c99d898c4a429b35f6fe6c9df771e1c980510 and https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/commit/89ca3446cc8dd71ed42cf79e9c3fa1d87663d1d8 have been implemented if Pulp Smash had to worry about the linting status of every plugin? I don't think so.

> > Pulp Smash is a non-trivial application. It's reasonable to think that its requirements and pulp_ansible's requirements might conflict, meaning that installing them into the same virtualenv won't work.
>
> Yes, agreed. This is something we'll have to solve although I think running pulp-smash tests against each PR would hopefully reveal such problems as they arise.

Perhaps the solution for this issue is to just cross those bridges when we come to them. There's significant benefit in allowing third parties to independently write tests, and it justifies some extra work. Also, I'd like to see one or two dependencies (plumbum!) dropped from Pulp Smash. This is a good motivating reason for accelerating that work.

Hi Phil!

The reason this is happening is because the DataLoader runs on top of the [promise loop engine](https://github.com/syrusakbary/promise/blob/master/promise/async_.py) instead of one based in gevent event loop.
So, the fields are resolved asynchronously, but the dataloader relies on the "next tick" of the event loop, which in this case is not Gevent's and therefore the ticks are triggered even before the Gevent next loop tick.

## Why creating an event loop?
In older versions of Python (2.7, 3.3) there was no event loop provided by default (asyncio was added in Python 3.4), and the easier way to have `Promise`s working in a universal way was to mimic the effects of an event loop.

## How to solve it
The way for fixing this is quite simple, by just making dataloader to push the job for batching to the Gevent loop instead of the custom one (`async_instance`).
https://github.com/syrusakbary/promise/blob/master/promise/dataloader.py#L199

I can assist you on that, if you need so!

I have some code which does some intelligent selective application of database optimisations. This version was written against graphene 0.X and is presented without any kind of guarantee. As and when I miraculously have time to work on it, I'd love to formalise this and get it working in a general case, but I've been saying that for about 9 months now so don't hold out hope.

The core idea is to add `prefetch_FOO` and `optimize_FOO` methods to your `DjangoObjectType`. The code will always do `prefetch_related`, even on FKs, and that will be applied automatically if applicable. The prefetched qs can be customised with the `prefetch_FOO` method, and further customised by `optimize_FOO` methods on the next `DjangoObjectType`. All optimisations are applied selectively - they won't be applied if the graphql query does not require that data.

Note that it also makes use of https://github.com/photocrowd/django-cursor-pagination

Questions to the package maintainers:

At the Django side, the bug concerning `{% include %}` and `{% extend %}` indeed exists in versions 1.9 and 1.10, and is being fixed in 1.11 LTS. However, this bugfix will not be backported to 1.9 and 1.10 (most probably because they will be out of support soon, according to Django roadmap). References: tickets [27584](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/27584) and [27956](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/27956).

At present, django-debug-toolbar copies the same code in Django to extract the template source. This code will need a small fix in order to work correctly in Django 1.11, but a more significant change will be required if we want it working with 1.9 and 1.10. You may compare the code of the [bugfix](https://github.com/django/django/pull/7686/files#diff-8cbe1fb6d589cb6e35b956704d7a1285R960) and [debug_toolbar/utils.py](https://github.com/jazzband/django-debug-toolbar/blob/master/debug_toolbar/utils.py).

The question is therefore: do we want this fix to only work with Django 1.11 LTS and keep how it is now with versions 1.9 and 1.10; or do we also provide another solution for these two older versions?

A 2nd question: do we need a unit test on this, or is it fine to just keep it the same with Django's code?

This is actually a thornier issue that I realized, and possibly the source of @ltauxe's problems with Thellier GUI.  In my code, I've tried to decrease the number of times we are reading in from csv files by passing the Contribution object around and updating it.  So right now, Pmag GUI writes to file often but reads from file rarely.  

I've refactored a bit to achieve the following: ba297513890e4a268b1f667c8782054b028370a3

- Pmag GUI now calls Thellier GUI and Demag GUI by creating a new frame instance, not by running main or start.  The Pmag GUI main frame is also now disabled while Thellier or Demag GUIs are running.

- Thellier GUI and Demag GUI main frames now both take an additional optional parameter, evt_quit.  This parameter is a custom event that fires when Thellier GUI or Demag GUI is closed.

- When Pmag GUI is pinged that Demag GUI or Thellier GUI have closed, it is re-enabled, shown, and also reads in any new data that might have been written to file by Demag or Thellier GUI.

Ideally, we wouldn't be reading in multiple times like this, but Demag and Thellier GUIs are both complicated and I think this is a good way (for now) to ensure that we don't miss things.  This update should also prevent some of the trouble @ltauxe had in #153, where I suspect Thellier GUI updated the files but not the Pmag GUI contribution object, thus resulting in some chaos.

The way to install PmagPy so that it is in the Python path is to use Pip install. Instructions for how to do so are found in the PmagPy cookbook here: https://earthref.org/PmagPy/cookbook/#QQ2-1-6

> **1.2.1 Installing PmagPy**

> Here we provide instructions for using pip to install PmagPy from the command line. “Pip” is a package manager that comes standard with any modern Python distribution (NB: you will have to update it to the most recent version). Pip allows you to install and uninstall pmagpy, the package with all the low-level functionality of PmagPy and pmagpy-cli which provides the PmagPy command-line-interface and has all the packages you may use to download/upload, visualize, and manipulate data.
To install using pip:

> Find and open your command line.
> Update pip: type on the command line:
> `pip install --upgrade pip`
> Install or update pmagpy: use the command:
> `pip install --upgrade pmagpy`
> Install or update pmagpy-cli, use the command:
> `pip install --upgrade pmagpy-cli` 
> To uninstall either, use the command: “pip uninstall pmagpy” or “pip uninstall pmagpy-cli”
> If you run into trouble, use pip to uninstall both pmagpy and pmagpy-cli, then try again to install first pmagpy and then pmagpy-cli

The other way to go is to add a statement such as:
`export PYTHONPATH=$PYTHONPATH:~/PmagPy` 
in your .profile or .bash_profile file that points to where PmagPy is on your local machine. Since it is in your home directory you should be able to put it into your .bash_profile as I have it above and the command line programs will then be able to find the pmagpy module.

This issue come from the model serialization. On serialization, `tensorflow` try to get the `name` property for a `list` or `dict` value.

There are severals places where a `list` or `dict` is added as collection value :
- https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/layers/recurrent.py#L92
- https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/layers/recurrent.py#L400
- https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/summaries.py#L51

[Here][1] `tensorflow` call the `name` property which is good for `Tensor` but not for `list` or `dict`. But the [_get_kind_type][2] does not make the distinction between `list or dict` and `Tensor`. 

We can maybe make a workaround with `tf.identity` ? @aymericdamien what do you think ? It's a misuse in `tflearn` or an issue for `tensorflow` ?

[1]: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/73ced9d797056c7e67a06ed2098dd809d85ec44a/tensorflow/python/training/saver.py#L1217
[2]: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/73ced9d797056c7e67a06ed2098dd809d85ec44a/tensorflow/python/training/saver.py#L1166-L1185

Hi @sauravm8,

The fastText supervised model works as follow: each word (and word ngram) is associated to a vector representation (a.k.a. embedding, in dimension 100 by default). A representation for the input text is obtained by averaging the embeddings corresponding to the words and ngrams that appear in the input. Then, a linear classifier is used on this representation to obtain the score corresponding to each label.

Thus, the average of the word/ngram vectors can be seen as a "hidden representation" of the sentence.

Best,
Edouard.

There is something fundamentally screwy with those two unit tests and pytest.

The following ways of running pytest all work fine:
```Bash
py.test
py.test -n2
py.test -n2 --forked
py.test -n2 --cov
```

However, with the following ways of running py.test, those two tests fail:
```Bash
py.test -k interrupt
py.test --forked
py.test --cov
```

Fixing whatever is wrong with those tests would be ideal, but I don't see anything wrong.  It may be that you just can't easily/reliably unit test signal handlers with pytest.  I suspect there is some way we could do it, but it might be difficult and time consuming to do it right.

I can see two easy solutions to move forward:
* Use ``pytest-xdist`` and **-n2** to run the tests when using --cov
* Remove those two unit tests

I'd be happy with either solution.

@patstew So, it looks like the cases are:

1. `T` is a non-structured type. In this case you can compare format strings, and even handle some platform-dependent weirdness manually (like `i` and `l` on Windows). Maybe it'd make sense to have a `normalize_scalar_format()` function that would handle all that instead of a comparison function? With a bit of effort, you could probably even make checks like `sizeof(int) == sizeof(long)` compile-time.

2. `T` is a structured type (basically, format string is not a single character?). Currently, you can only register those via `PYBIND11_NUMPY_DTYPE`, so we know that NumPy is present, and we can compare dtypes for equivalence, namely `py::dtype::of<T>()` and dtype constructed from buffer info object.

Note that you could still use NumPy dtype comparison in (1), but if comparing format strings for simple scalars is sufficient then it's fine.

Adding to bmw's points, it's also the case that `certbot-auto` checks hashes for everything it downloads with pip.  That _doesn't_ mean there's an audit against malicious code in any of those Python packages, but it does mean that a transient MITM or compromise against `pypi.python.org` can't slip code in that's different to whatever was observed during the Certbot release process.

The deeper problem ("how do we ensure that there's nothing fatally malicious in any of the open source projects that many of our systems depend on") is going to take a much wider effort amongst the open source community to solve.

In case it helps, `exec_command` is using `subprocess.Popen()` and `.communicate()` on that, and the [Python 3 `subprocess.Popen.communicate` man page](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen.communicate) notes that `out` will be a `str` if the streams were opened in `text` mode, and `bytes` otherwise.  [`exec_command`](https://github.com/FPGAwars/apio/blob/aff504f95e8169f65155c87e0562aaabec3e4cd4/apio/util.py#L307-L339) appears to leave the stream mode (text/binary) up to the caller, and [`get_tinyprog_meta`](https://github.com/FPGAwars/apio/blob/aff504f95e8169f65155c87e0562aaabec3e4cd4/apio/util.py#L441-L446) does not do anything to make it text (so my guess is at least on Linux/Unix Python will default to binary/bytes).  The [`Popen` constructor](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#popen-constructor) takes a `text=True` argument, which might be sufficient to fix this issue.

[Before Python 3.6, `json.loads()` expects to receive a `str` *not* `bytes`/`bytearray`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/json.html#basic-usage) -- from Python 3.6 onwards, it will also accept `bytes`/`bytearray`.  So this appears to be an "older Python 3.x" compatibility issue -- Python 2.7, and Python 3.6+ seem likely to work.  Debian Stretch and Ubuntu 16.04 both use Python 3.5, which *is* affected by this.  (Eg, [Debian Stretch python3 package](https://packages.debian.org/stretch/python3).)

That perhaps explains why relatively few users are affected -- Python 3.6 has enough backwards compatibility updates to make it work, and the TinyFPGA BX example is Python 3.6....

In theory `text=True` passed through the chain to `Popen` would be sufficient to make it work everywhere for this specific use case

In the minor version Pyarmro 3.3.0, there are 2 new modes: 7, 8. The default mode is 8.

* mode 7: only code object of python module is obfuscated, it's almost
  quickly as no obfuscated module.
  
```
    python pyarmor.py --with-capsule=project.zip --output=dist --mode=7 foo.py
    
```

* mode 8: both code object and bytecode are obfuscated, it's slower
  but more secure. It's the default mode.

```
    python pyarmor.py --with-capsule=project.zip --output=dist --mode=8 foo.py

```

Both of modes generate obfuscated scripts as the following:

```
__pyarmor__(__name__, b'xxxx')
```

I think it's efficient and safe enough only clear these 2 frames. For example,
```python
def foo(filename):
    # It will raise OSError if pass a directory
    os.remove(filename)

@wraparmor
def level3(filename):
    foo(filename)

@wraparmor
def level2(filename):
    level3(filename)

@wraparmor
def level1(filename):
    level2(filename)
```
In outer script, call `level1`
```python
    level1('/')
```
It will raise OSError in frame `os.remove`. The frames of `level1`, `level2`, `level3` are `tp_clear`ed, `foo` and all the inner frame are not. Even if adding an extra flag to `foo`, all the inner frames of system library are still original. Besides, if iterating all the inner frames, it means many duplicated `tp_clear` in the same frame when `__wraparmor__(func, tb, 1)` is called:

* level3: clear nothing
* level2: clear `level3`
* level1: clear `level2`, `level3` (duplicated call)

So the ruler is to add decorator to any important function in python scripts.

The easiest thing to do would be to use [our Terraform Bridge](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-terraform) to adapt the existing [Terraform Nomad Provider](https://github.com/terraform-providers/terraform-provider-nomad).  The bridge repo's [README](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-terraform/blob/master/README.md) has high-level instructions for how this work, and the [Pulumi AWS](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws) repo is a good blueprint for how this would work (albeit quite a bit more complex than many other providers).  Basically, create a new repo with the two requisite commands (a code-generator and a plugin), make a [`resources.go`](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-aws/blob/master/resources.go) that maps the resource types to nice programming language names, and wire up the right build commands.

TBH, I wish our docs here were better, so if you want to help make them easier to follow as you go, that would be much appreciated too!  But no worries if you'd rather focus on the code.  Judging by early interest, I suspect lots of people will want to do this in the weeks to come, that's all 😄

The problem is gone now, and I'm having trouble recreating the original problem. The things I tried, one of which seems to have worked, were the following:

1. `pip install -U protobuf==3.0.0b2 --user` (I got the idea from tensorflow/tensorflow#1402).
2. Make sure to add the right version of Python to my PATH environment variable inside of the script for installing TensorFlow on all the nodes (see https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/doc/using-ray-on-a-large-cluster.md).
3. Instead of installing TensorFlow with `pip install tensorflow`, do
```
export TF_BINARY_URL=https://storage.googleapis.com/tensorflow/linux/cpu/tenso\
rflow-0.12.1-cp27-none-linux_x86_64.whl
pip install --ignore-installed --upgrade --user $TF_BINARY_URL
```

Yes, I also found that it only breaks in Jupyter. Keras uses carriage returns to update the progress bar, so when it runs code like this (yes, for some reason Keras prints the progress bar in two `write`s):

    sys.stdout.write('[===>    ]')
    sys.stdout.write(' some metric = 7')
    sys.stdout.flush()
    sys.stdout.write('\r)
    sys.stdout.write('[====>   ]')
    sys.stdout.write(' some metric = 8')
    sys.stdout.flush()

We expect the printout to be `[===>    ] some metric = 7`  then `[====>   ] some metric = 8`. With colorama, the second write after the carriage return gets moved to the end of the line (somehow, probably a colorama bug). It ends up semi-overlapped, like `[===>    ] some metric = 7 some metric = 8`, which obviously gets overwhelming as training goes on.

I think using colorama is fine, it's just a bit of a surprise/hard to debug if you don't know it's being used. Would running `colorama.deinit()` break anything? If not, then just documenting the fact that it's used would suffice, since anyone who doesn't want it could just disable it.

I like the name "nursery". If we choose a new one, I'd hope that it share some of the properties off "nursery" that make me like it:
- not in common usage elsewhere (as mentioned, maybe we can do even better than "nursery" in this regard, but I don't think the GC term overloading is a substantial issue)
- short and easy to type (I think seven characters is substantially better even than the ten in "splitpoint")
- doesn't invite ambiguous abbreviations (`mgr` could be a ThingManager for just about any value of Thing, and a large program might have a lot of those)
- reads well in the trio pattern of `open_thing()` being a function that returns a context manager that scopes the lifetime of a new `thing`
- not _too_ cutesy (I think "party" might be going a bit too far in that direction)
- suits the context in which it's used reasonably well - it doesn't have to be obvious without an explanation, but the explanation should make sense

I'm not sure it makes sense to switch for a name that's only infinitesimally better than "nursery", since there are switching costs. I don't feel like any of the names proposed so far are substantially better than "nursery", though I do like several of them.

Some random further thoughts:
- "custodian" is in common usage in Scheme, I think, and refers to a sort of scope for _all_ resources - when a custodian is shut down, all files created in its scope are closed, threads are killed, etc. IMO it's similar enough to the nursery idea to make the differences a potential point of confusion for Schemers.
- The unit-of-an-organization meaning of "chapter" doesn't jump out at me nearly as much as the book-subdivision one does, so it feels like a mixed metaphor - the instinctive objection is "my program isn't made of chapters, that's for books! programs are made of (modules|packages|functions|...)". Nursery doesn't have this problem because nobody talks about dividing things into nurseries, in any domain.
- I quite like "hive" and "nest", and they're very short and non-overloaded (as long as no one tries to write a trio library for controlling a certain trendy home thermostat...), but I think they score worse on "likely preexisting associations link to correct concepts" than nursery does.

Excellent question!

So, one of Trio's weirdest, most controversial decisions is that it takes the position that the existence of a background task is *not* an implementation detail, and *should* be exposed as part of your API. On balance I think this is the right decision, but it's definitely a bit experimental and has some trade-offs.

Why does Trio do this? In my experience, other systems make it seem like you can abstract away the presence of a background task or thread, but in reality it leaks in all kinds of ways: they end up breaking control-C handling, or they cause problems when you're trying to exit the program cleanly, or they leak when you try to cancel the main operation, or you have sequencing problems because the function you called completed but the work it promised to do is still going on in the background, or the background task crashes with an unexpected exception and then the exception gets lost and all kinds of weird problems ensue... so while it might make your API feel a little messier in the short term, in the long term everything goes easier if you make this explicit.

Also, keep in mind that everyone else writing and using Trio libraries has the same issue, so your API is not going to feel too weird :-).

I don't know what you're trying to do exactly. Maybe it's something like a websocket connection, where you want to constantly be reading from the socket to respond to heartbeat ("ping") requests. One pattern would be to do something like:

```python
@asynccontextmanager
def open_websocket(url):
    ws = WebSocket()
    await ws._connect(url)
    try:
        async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
            nursery.start_soon(ws._heartbeat_task)
            yield ws
            # Cancel the heartbeat task, since we're about to close the connection
            nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
    finally:
        await ws.aclose()
```

And then your users can use it like:

```python
async with open_websocket("https://...") as ws:
    await ws.send("hello")
    ...
```

If you want to get fancier, another option would be to provide one version where your users pass in their own nursery, for experts:

```python
class WebSocket(trio.abc.AsyncResource):
    def __init__(self, nursery, url):
        self._nursery = nursery
        self.url = url

    async def connect(self):
        # set up the connection
        ...
        # start the heartbeat task
        self._nursery.start_soon(self._heartbeat_task)

    async def aclose(self):
        # you'll need some way to shut down the heartbeat task here
        ...
```

and then also provide a convenience API, for those who just want one connection and don't want to mess with nurseries:

```python
@asynccontextmanager
async def open_websocket(url):
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        async with WebSocket(nursery, url) as ws:
            await ws.connect()
            yield ws
```

The main advantage of the pass-in-a-nursery approach is that if your users want to open lots of websocket connections, an arbitrary number of websocket connections, then they can open one nursery once at the top of their websocket management code, and then have lots of websockets inside it.

You're probably wondering, though: where do you find this `@asynccontextmanager`? Well, it's included in the stdlib in 3.7, but that's not even out yet, so you're probably not using it. I guess we need something better. Probably the solution is to take the version from the 3.7 stdlib, and put it into the [`async_generator`](https://github.com/njsmith/async_generator) library. If you'd like to do a PR for that, then excellent :-). Otherwise I'll get around to it as soon as I can...

The problem was with how IPOPT opens the IPOPT.out file and writes to it when the printlevel >= 1. The IPOPT documentation says that printlevel can be between 0 and 10.
    printlevel = diaglevel*2
    printlevel = MIN(MAX(printlevel,0),10)
    ierr = ipopenoutputfile(iproblem, 'ipopt.out', printlevel)
This works in Windows (g95 compiler, IPOPT 3.10.2) but apparently there is a problem with Linux (ifort, IPOPT 3.12.8). I'm not sure if it is the compiler, the version of IPOPT, or the OS. I just set the print level to zero for IPOPT to get rid of this bug for Linux.
    printlevel = 0  ! print level >=1 crashes Linux
    ierr = ipopenoutputfile(iproblem, 'ipopt.out', printlevel)
This unfortunately removes a feature of IPOPT to allow more detailed diagnostics of the solver but does fix the crash bug. Public servers (Linux) and Windows executable with GEKKO is updated with the fix.

@jackvreeken @jbaayen I went down a rabbit hole trying to find a clean way to do make sure we have a consistent AST and validate it. I investigated writing code generation for the AST bindings, then looked into binding libraries. I wasn't happy with anything python offered, but found a very interesting BSD compatible C++ library (https://www.codesynthesis.com/products/xsd). This led me to try out the antlr C++ runtime with our grammar. It is kind of tempting.

## Cymoca
* https://github.com/jgoppert/cymoca
 
### Upside
* The AST is completely generated from the ModelicaXML.xsd
* The parser is completely genereated from Modelica.g4
* There is only one class we have to write, that does the mapping between the two, very little code maintenance. 
* This code looks very similar to our python backend. https://github.com/jgoppert/cymoca/blob/master/src/cymoca/Compiler.cpp#L53
* Faster compilation, lower memory footprint
* Bidirectional XML read/write, but stored as a C++ class.
* It is impossible to generate a non-valid ModelicaXML document, since the AST is a direct mapping.
* A python binding like Casadi uses would be possible.

### Downside
* Changing languages is a pretty huge deal, so I'm not sure how rapidly we could port existing code.
* I'm not sure how this works for your use-case
* C++ can be less hack-able, requires more setup time.
